So a couple of things-

First, for the centrifuge database that you created, the location you should enter is:

`/home/name/scripts/centrifuge_database/abv`

Second, do you have centrifuge installed? A quick way to check is by running:

`dRep bonus test --check_dependencies`

Finally, when you try this again, include the `-d` option when running the program. This will make some extra output that will make it easier to figure out what the problem is if these things don't fix it.

You can can also try this again without re-running the whole program by just using the command:

`dRep bonus /home/output_dir/ --run_tax --cent_index /home/name/scripts/centrifuge_database/abv -d`

That command will just try the bonus step again on the genome provided during your last run.

Best,
-Matt

Like Duff's Device, this example is some sort of argument in this debate, but I'm not sure whether it's for or against.

You have a huge project made up of a bunch modules, including database.py, webservice.py, and utils.py. You add test code to utils.py such that running it will do all your unit testing of that module. You add code to database.py that uses Clize to allow you to call up certain database actions via the back end. Does it make sense to have Clize  Running webservice.py fires up the main server, which (of necessity) imports both the others. Is it right to have Clize "active" for all the database.py functions, when they got imported from webservice?

Pro: It would mean that your main routine could provide functionality from all over the project, very easily, simply by importing stuff that uses the decorator. (Compare how Flask works - you can import Flask from multiple places, get the same `app` object in all of them, and use `@app.route("/")` in one file and `@app.route("/api/blah")` in a completely different file, and the same server then has human-readable and machine-readable endpoints.)

Con: It'd be difficult to consciously separate multiple uses of Clize within one project.

Is it more likely that you'd want a simple way to Clize more than once in a single package, or that you'd want a simple way to Clize something that can be imported from elsewhere?

in case this helps anyone else, I found this example in the ["Connecting to Broker](http://rubyamqp.info/articles/connecting_to_broker/) guide of the Ruby AMPQ Gem:

```
The vhost is obtained from the first segment of the path, with the leading slash removed. The path should contain only a single segment (i.e, the only slash in it should be the leading one). If the vhost is to include slashes or other reserved URI characters, these should be percent-escaped.

Here are some examples that demonstrate how AMQP::Client.parse_connection_uri parses out the vhost from connection URIs:

"amqp://dev.rabbitmq.com"            # => vhost is nil, so default ("/") will be used
"amqp://dev.rabbitmq.com/"           # => vhost is an empty string
"amqp://dev.rabbitmq.com/%2Fvault"   # => vhost is "/vault"
"amqp://dev.rabbitmq.com/production" # => vhost is "production"
"amqp://dev.rabbitmq.com/a.b.c"      # => vhost is "a.b.c"
"amqp://dev.rabbitmq.com/foo/bar"    # => ArgumentError
```

I have confirmed this with my own system.

Given a vhost of `zzz` you have to pass `/zzz` as the vhost name in the ampq URI, so you actually wind up URL-encoding that and passing `%2Fzzz` like this:

```
amqp://{user}@{pass}:dev.rabbitmq.com/%2Fzzz
```

which creates or accesses queues in the `/zzz` virtual host.

@brettcannon: IIUC, WSGI does two things: (a) it provides a standard interface between servers (gunicode / mod_wsgi / ...) and frameworks for implementing response handlers (django / flask / ...), so you can mix and match them and write generic middleware, and (b) it bakes in a particular concurrency model where web response handlers are called in a synchronous blocking fashion. Part (a) is useful regardless of whether different event loops can get along, because even within each event loop universe probably people will write more than one web app and would prefer not to have to implement their own HTTP server from scratch each time. Part (b) though makes WSGI itself kinda useless in the curio/asyncio context, because response handlers need to be able to invoke arbitrary I/O of their own (e.g. fetching results from a database before generating a web page). So in the curio/asyncio context, you need to pass the loop into the response handlers, and unless you manage to make all event loops 100% interoperable then this user code is going to be bound to a particular event loop.

I'm probably going to regret ever suggesting this, but I'm now thinking that three exceptions might be needed here.  Consider this scenario:

     try:
        async with timeout_after(100):
            async with timeout_after(5):
                   await curio.sleep(10)
     except TaskTimeout:
        print('It took too long')

The inner timeout will fire and create a `TaskTimeout` exception.  However, that exception isn't being caught properly.  Instead, it propagates out to the enclosing 100s timeout.   This creates an interesting dilemma.  Is the outer context supposed to receive a `TaskTimeout` even though its time interval hasn't expired?   

I'm somewhat inclined to say no.  Instead, maybe it should receive some kind of operational error `UncaughtTimeoutError` that indicates the presence of a timeout, but no code to actually catch the error at the point of failure. 

Short version:   You will only get a `TaskTimeout` if your time interval has actually expired.

Many of the parameters from `minialign` will have corresponding parameters in `minimap2`, so you could theoretically compare the preset value for `map-ont` with those.

`-I` is an indexing option. Once you create the index, you can't change the parameter (see also the man page). Minimap2 gives a warning if build-time `-k`, `-w` and `-H` are different from map-time values. However, it is hard to check the consistency between the build-time `-I` and the map-time `-I` because different `-I` values may still yield the same index. Wow, I have just realized a second thing. We must distinguish two cases:

1. the library (static or shared, no matter) contains only non-module procedures;
2. the library contains modules.

In the first case no `.mod` files are required (neither created...), but in the second case they are necessary. Moreover, a library, both `.a` or `.so`, does not never contain the `.mod` files! This brings us to the case where, if all the library's procedures are encapsulated into modules, the `.a` or `.so` are irrelevant! For compile a source that has a `use` statement the only relevant files are the `.mod`. The static and shared library are useful *only* for pack together non-module-encapsulated procedures!

> 5. Creates .pyc files as part of installation to ensure they match the python interpreter used.

Original comment by: Dmitry Shachnev

Yes, I have nothing against the priorities mechanism.

> And that is essentially what we have - kwallet.DBusBackend is 5.1, SecretService is 5.0, Gnome is 1.0, and other backends are generally lower, though some are not, such as pyfs which is 2.0.

In my opinion GNOME should have higher priority than pyfs. Or at least equal.

> Problem is, what about kwallet.QtKeyring? Should it be 5.0? Maybe it should also be downgraded to 1.0.

I agree about downgrading it, provided that QtKeyring uses Qt 4 which is EOL upstream (since December).

> What if secretstorage is available but not a suitable version?

I think Keyring works with 1.0, which was the first stable version.

> Keyring attempts to solve this problem (or more accurately avoid explicitly solving the problem) by allowing each of the backends to independently declare their relative viability. Original comment by: Gurce Isikyildiz

Let me see if I understand correctly. You are prompted for a password during each Mercurial operation, but you would like keyring not to have to prompt you for the master password every time. Is that correct?

Assuming that is so, there is no secure way to achieve what you desire. Each invocation of Mercurial creates a new, unique invocation of Python. That invocation of Python has no state or knowledge about previously-entered passwords, and if there was, it would allow an attacker to compromise your entire keyring. Just imagine, how would keyring know how to decrypt the password file without the master password?

For this reason, the PyCrypto keyring only provides value in keeping multiple credentials stored behind a single master password, but the master password must necessarily be entered at every invocation of the process.

For this reason, the PyCrypto keyring is not particularly well-suited for something like Mercurial where the goal is not to retype the password on every command.

I suggest instead you use one of the recommended, platform-backed front ends, which will leverage your operating-system level credentials to authorize you for your passwords, keeping them secure, but exposing them to the application for the duration of your session.

Most mesh formats are single precision- PLY supports double precision technically but the implementation in trimesh exports vertices as 32 bit floats,  as most files you see in the wild are 32 bit. @mahmoud Yes, the code examples were updated. See this part in the `LocalTimezone` example:

```python
        # Detect fold
        fold = (args == _time.localtime(stamp - dst_diff))
        return datetime(*args, microsecond=dt.microsecond,
                        tzinfo=self, fold=fold)
```

`fold` was not a keyword argument before Python 3.6.

> Seems to suggest they don't think they need fold?

Completely unambiguous time zones (zones with no DST ever) don't need to handle fold because no folds occur in those zones. If you just need a "point in time" you can also use `timezone` or `dateutil.tz.tzoffset()` (I think there's a bolton called `FixedOffset` maybe?), because that is a single fixed offset with no knowledge of DST - essentially equivalent to UTC.

I think what they are basically saying is that if you have an application where being wrong about the time during DST transitions is not a big issue, you don't have to bother with handling the fold. As a library developer, though, it's probably best to have your library do the same thing, since most people won't realize the library they are using has an edge case until they run into it.

I'd say the simplest solution is to just opportunistically use/set the `fold` information - if you provide a `datetime` object that has a `fold` attribute, use it, otherwise assume it is 0. Users can upgrade to Python 3.6+ if they want the correct behavior, or can select a library like `dateutil` (or, for a more all-inclusive solution, something like `pendulum`), which includes a backport.

If you want the behavior to be consistent across versions, you can always just import the `dateutil` backport solution (which is in turn based on Alexander Beloposky's MIT-licensed backport).

Without the actual code I cannot say too much, but the package didn't change on API on the `trend()`. The function `pybedtools.helpers.cleanup` is registered to be [run on exit](https://github.com/daler/pybedtools/blob/master/pybedtools/helpers.py#L702) but it looks like it's not getting triggered after exiting the `multiprocess`-created process. After looking into it, this is a [known issue with `multiprocessing`](http://stackoverflow.com/questions/26452713/python-process-which-is-joined-will-not-call-atexit).

I'm not sure there's a general, internal-to-pybedtools solution. But the easy solution in this case is to explicitly call `pybedtools.helpers.cleanup()` in `main()`. So the following won't leave any tempfiles around:

```python
#!/usr/bin/python
import pybedtools
import os
import multiprocessing

os.environ['TMPDIR'] = './'

def main():
    regions = [('chr2', int(1), int(2), 'tag')]
    b = pybedtools.BedTool(regions)
    pybedtools.helpers.cleanup()

p = multiprocessing.Process(target=main)
p.start()
p.join()
```

Maybe we should revert this change and do something like this instead:

1) have set `PSUTIL_TESTING=1` env variable in the Makefile for the `make test*` target.
2) from `setup.py` install `psutil.tests` package only if that variable is set

    pip install --upgrade numpy

try:

my suggestion is that we receive parameters sent to telepot methods as a dictionary to `__init__` of our custom exception along with name of method , so we can create our own custom properties.

```python
class BotWasKickedError(TelegramError):
    def __init__(self, description, error_code, params):
        super(BotWasKickedError).__init__(self, description, error_code, params)
        self.chat_id= params['chat_id']
    DESCRIPTION_PATTERNS = ['bot.*blocked']
```

Because it's not getting the right player. There are multiple players in that game with the name `J.Jones`. If you do:

```python
print DE1.player
print DE1.formatted_stats()
```

Then you can see what's really going on:

```
Jacoby Jones (WR, SD)
kickret_yds: 24, kickret_ret: 1, puntret_fair: 2

I don't know how to give you a fix because you didn't tell us what problem you're trying to solve. If you're trying to "look up player stats by name," then you are trying to solve a **hard problem**. Many players share the same name, so you typically need other mechanisms to narrow down the search, such as team and position. Relatedly, sometimes names are spelled slightly differently than you expect, which means you need some kind of fuzzy match.

[`nfldb`](https://github.com/BurntSushi/nfldb) gives you a lot more tools for player lookup, including fuzzy search and narrowing down players by position/team. There are examples here: https://github.com/BurntSushi/nfldb/wiki/Fuzzy-player-name-matching (This kind of functionality will probably never be in `nflgame`.)

@jacksonofalltrades Can you provide a code snippet which recreates this issue? I'm assuming that you are trying to change the PatientName field or something by assigning a unicode string?

In the meantime, you can perform the assignment in the way that it was intended:

```
from dicom.valuerep import PersonName
dataset.PatientName = PersonName(u'Adams^Sam')
```

This works assuming that you don't have any non-latin characters in your name. If you need additional encodings for the other components (idiographic and phonetic), then you will need to use PersonNameUnicode and specify a list of encodings as the second input. Note that if you have to do this, you need to also modify the SpecificCharacterSet to match.

What I found out after 4.0.3 is that different databases return the column names differently.  SQL Server, PostgreSQL, and MySQL all return them as UTF-16LE regardless of the data encoding.

I've added a 3rd decoding option for this but defaulted it to utf-16le.  Please add this:

    connection.setdecoding(pyodbc.SQL_WMETADATA, encoding='utf-8')

Sorry for the trouble, but each database does things different instead of following the spec.  (In this case the spec says it *should* be UTF16LE.).

I'm considering creating a "factory" that can be configured and then connections created using factory.connect().  I'd also consider adding something like:

    factory.autoconfigure('teradata')

with setting people have tested.  (I don't have teradata.)

In the meantime I'll try to keep the docs up to date.  Please let me know if this works for you.

This reference explains the Wynns epsilon algorithm in detail:

   - http://arxiv.org/pdf/math/0306302v1.pdf


It may happen that the difference between values in the epsilon table
vanishes. If pathologies of that kind occur, the so-called singular rules
of the epsilon algorithm can be used which were also derived by Wynn [39].
A good discussion of these singular rules and of related problems can also
be found in section 4.1.2 of Brezinski’s second book. (Brezinski,
Algorithmes d’acc´el´eration de la convergence – Etude num´erique ´
(Editions Technip, Paris, 1978).)

I'm reopening this, as pychromecast 0.8.0 is out, and catt is no longer working properly. The new issue is not related to what we already discussed (get_chromecast), but rather with how `play_media` now works internally.
Please see https://github.com/balloob/pychromecast/issues/151.
The problem with `block_until_active()` is that it is implemented in such a way, that it is blocking until `media_controller` is actually playing something. That means we can't use it in `CastController.init`. And as described in the pychromecast issue, this method call is now needed to make `play_media` work properly. 
I'll discuss this with the pychromecast people. In the meantime I can make a PR with a freeze on requirements, if you like.

```python
import contextlib

Through trial and error I think I figured it out:

```
from pynput import keyboard
from pynput import mouse
import os

# get initial capslock state
exit_code = os.system('''xset q 2>/dev/null | grep -q -E 'Caps Lock: +on' ''')
if exit_code == 0:
	capslock = True
else:
	capslock = False

# this function runs each time a key is pressed
def on_press(key): 
	global capslock
	output = str(key)
	if output == 'Key.caps_lock': # if capslock pressed, swap capslock state
		capslock = not capslock
	if output.startswith('Key') == False: # i.e., if output is alphanumeric
		output = output[1:-1] # remove quotes around character
		if capslock:
			output = output.swapcase()
	if output.startswith('Key.shift') == False: # i.e., ignore shift keys (e.g., we want to log a~ not aKey.shift~)
		print(output)

def on_click(x, y, button, pressed):
	button = str(button)
	if pressed:
		if button == 'Button.left':
			print('Key.mousebutton_left')

with mouse.Listener(on_click=on_click) as listener:
	with keyboard.Listener(on_press=on_press) as listener:
		listener.join()
```

The `form_hidden_fields` dict is just turned into some hidden fields in a very straightforward and uninteresting way, (see https://github.com/plumdog/flask_table/blob/v0.5.0/flask_table/columns.py#L342-L349).

So your code above will become
```html
<form method="post">
    <input type="hidden" name="csrf_token" value="{{ csrf_token() }}"/>
</form>
```
in the actual HTML - no templating happens on the values you pass. So what you'll need to do is - in effect, call `csrf_token()` in Python, not in the template.

I suspect if you do
```python
form_hidden_fields=dict(csrf_token=my_get_csrf_token_function())
```
it will work, but you need for `my_get_csrf_token_function()` to do (in Python) the same thing that your `csrf_token()` is doing in your template. I don't think there's a standard in Flask for CSRF stuff, but if you're using `FlaskWTF`, then I think the function you want is actually `flask_wtf.csrf.generate_csrf_token`, so it'd be:
```python
from flask_wtf.csrf import generate_csrf_token
...
form_hidden_fields=dict(csrf_token=generate_csrf_token())
```
You can check if it is doing the right thing by looking at the HTML it generates.

Hope that's useful! Let me know if anything is still not working for you.

Ok, looking forward to see this implemented. In case anyone is interested in testing multi-part form data, here's what I did:

Create a file with the multipart-form data. For example:

`--------------------------4935875673ad6222
Content-Disposition: form-data; name="username"

george
--------------------------4935875673ad6222
Content-Disposition: form-data; name="file"; filename="image.jpg"
Content-Type: image/jpeg
Content-Transfer-Encoding: base64

/9j/4AAQSkZJRgABAQAAAQABAAD/2wBDA <rest of base64 string>
--------------------------4935875673ad6222--
`
Set the appropriate headers and load the data into the body of the request.
`- test:
  - group: "Group test"
  - name: "Test name"
  - url: "/endpoint"
  - method: "POST"
  - headers: {'Content-Type': 'multipart/form-data; boundary=------------------------4935875673ad6222'}
  - body: {'file':'formdata.txt'}
  - expected_status: [200]`

You cannot modify the menu object once the systray icon is created. This is by design.

You can however let the menu be a callable returning the sequence of menu items depending on your application state. See [the documentation](http://pystray.readthedocs.io/en/latest/usage.html#creating-the-menu) for an example: the code sample for *radio* items contains a dynamic example.

Finished a really crappily coded version of the Weekly interval trigger. 
Its a complete toy but appears to do the job for me.

===============================


from apscheduler.triggers.base import BaseTrigger
import pytz
from datetime import date
from datetime import datetime
from datetime import timedelta


class WeeklyTrigger(BaseTrigger):

    def __init__ (self, weekinterval, daysofweek, hourofday, timezone):
        """
        print "WeekInterval: " + str(weekinterval) + ", DaysOfWeek: " + \
        str(daysofweek) + ", HourOfDay: " + str(hourofday)
        """
        self.HourOfDay = hourofday
        self.WeeklyInterval = weekinterval
        self.DaysOfWeek = []
        self.timezone = timezone
        dowtokens = daysofweek.split (",")
        for dow in dowtokens:
            if dow == "mon":
                self.DaysOfWeek.append (0)
            elif dow == "tue":
                self.DaysOfWeek.append (1)
            elif dow == "wed":
                self.DaysOfWeek.append (2)
            elif dow == "thu":
                self.DaysOfWeek.append (3)
            elif dow == "fri":
                self.DaysOfWeek.append (4)
            elif dow == "sat":
                self.DaysOfWeek.append (5)
            elif dow == "sun":
                self.DaysOfWeek.append (6)
            else:
                raise Exception ("Invalid day of week: " + str(dow))





    def calculate_fire_day_of_week (self, fireweek, dow, starttime):
        """
        within the week where firing is needed, find the first day of the
        week when we should fire.
        """
        fireweek_dow = date.weekday (fireweek)
        #print "fireweek_dow: " + str(fireweek_dow) + ", dow: " + str(dow)
        scheddiff = abs (fireweek_dow - dow)
        #print "scheddiff: " + str(scheddiff)
        if fireweek_dow > dow:
            fireday = fireweek - timedelta (days = scheddiff)
        elif fireweek_dow < dow:
            fireday = fireweek + timedelta (days = scheddiff)
        else:
            fireday = fireweek

        #print "fireday: " + str(fireday)
        return fireday





    def calculate_week_interval (self, dow, starttime):

        """ sometimes the week when the event needs to be fired is not X weeks
        away but tomorrow, because the earliest day of week possible is within
        the current week.

        This method determines based on the dow and starttime(which can be
        previous_fire_time or now), whether the interval should be 0, in case
        the job needs to be fired this week, or if the job needs to be fired
        in self.WeeklyInterval time frame.
        """
        st_dow = date.weekday (starttime)
        if st_dow > dow:
            interval = self.WeeklyInterval
            #print "weekly interval check: st_dow > dow, Interval: " + str(interval)
        elif st_dow < dow:
            interval = 0
            #print "weekly interval check: st_dow < dow, Interval: " + str(interval)
        elif st_dow == dow:
            starttime_hour = starttime.hour
            if self.HourOfDay <= starttime_hour:
                interval = self.WeeklyInterval
            else:
                interval = 0
            #print "weekly interval check: st_dow == dow, Interval: " + str(interval)

        return interval





    def calculate_fire_time_for_dow (self, dow, starttime):

        """
        once the day of week,  in the selected week has been determined, for
        that day of week, calculate the hour of day when to fire the event.
        """
        weeklyinterval = self.calculate_week_interval (dow, starttime)
        #print "starttime: " + str(starttime)
        #print "weeklyinterval decision: " + str(weeklyinterval)
        fireweek = starttime + timedelta (weeks = weeklyinterval)
        #print "fireweek: " + str(fireweek)
        fireday = self.calculate_fire_day_of_week (fireweek, dow, starttime)
        #print "fireday: " + str(fireday)
        firetime = fireday.replace (hour = self.HourOfDay, minute = 0, second = 0, \
                                    microsecond = 0)
        #print "firetime: " + str(firetime)
        return firetime




    def calculate_next_fire_time (self, starttime):

        next_firetime = None
        """
        consider each day of week when the job needs to be run. find the
        timestamp for the earliest day of week when the job can be run.
        then among all the days of the week, select the one thats the
        earliest.
        """
        for dow in self.DaysOfWeek:
            firetime = self.calculate_fire_time_for_dow (dow, starttime)
            #print "firetime candidate: " + str(firetime)
            if next_firetime is None:
                next_firetime = firetime
            else:
                if firetime < next_firetime:
                    next_firetime = firetime

        return next_firetime





    def get_next_fire_time (self, previous_fire_time, now):

        if previous_fire_time:
            next_fire_time = self.calculate_next_fire_time (previous_fire_time)
        else:
            next_fire_time = self.calculate_next_fire_time (now)

        print str(next_fire_time)
        return next_fire_time





    def __str__(self):
        return "Week Interval: " + str(self.WeeklyInterval) \
               + ", DaysOfWeek: " + str(self.DaysOfWeek) \
               + ", HourOfDay: " + str (self.HourOfDay)

A couple of tweaks I'd like to see to make it more closely match the `@parameterized` style:

- Accept two arguments, parameter names, and then a list of parameters similar to those used by `@parameterized`
- Use the first parameter as the suffix (following the style of `@parameterized.expand`)

For example:

```python
@parameterize_class("username password", [
    ("user1", "1234"),
    ("user2", "5678"),
])
class TestUserAccounts(object):
    def test_login(self):
        assert can_login(self.username, self.password)
```

Hi @gavinhodge ,

Headers can be overriden in two ways:

1. In code, when defining a provider
2. At runtime, by arguments

You will see those two options in the constructor method of a Query (around [line 390](https://github.com/DenisCarriere/geocoder/blob/master/geocoder/base.py#L390) of base.py):

```
        # headers can be overriden in _build_headers
        self.headers = self._build_headers(provider_key, **kwargs).copy()
        self.headers.update(kwargs.get('headers', {}))
```

You have an example for [bing.py](https://github.com/DenisCarriere/geocoder/blob/master/geocoder/bing.py#L112) that will interest you:

```
    def _build_headers(self, provider_key, **kwargs):
        return {
            'Referer': "http://addxy.com/",
            'User-agent': 'Mozilla/5.0'
        }
```

Thanks for your suggestions. I absolutely understand your confusion with the documentation. However, in light of what is common and standard behavior for Unix tools – and what an argument parsing software can implement from a logical standpoint – I think it's is quite unambiguous and the behavior is well-defined.

Let me explain (slow Friday here at the office):

1. Remember that an option is always something *optional*. If you do not set an option, a default will be assumed. Of course, you must tell the user what that default is: it is shown in brackets in the documentation (i.e., `normalized`). Only when you want to change such a default, you set the option. This is how all well-behaving Unix tools work. (For example, see how [the `less` program](https://linux.die.net/man/1/less) tells you about what it does by default unless you set some option. Or, think about `git pull <url>` – it chooses unlimited depth for you, but you can also do `git pull --depth 0 <url>` to specify that you only want one commit.)

2. When you specify an option-argument in a utility documentation (e.g. `--prefix <prefix>`, with those brackets), the option-argument *must* be given by the user when he or she is specifying the option. There cannot be an option plus option-argument without its argument. You can check the [POSIX Utility Conventions](http://pubs.opengroup.org/onlinepubs/9699919799/basedefs/V1_chap12.html) for more info on that.

To summarize – and taking up your suggestion – there cannot be an option `--prefix` that specifies a default *and* an option `--prefix` that allows you to pass some other prefix. The argument parser must know if `--prefix` takes an option argument or not. It cannot be specified as both at the same time.

I would be hesitant to change the tool's behavior to remove the default `normalized` prefix, since it may break existing scripts (including some I have internally). The `--no-prefix` option should give you the possibility to tackle all use-cases, I hope.

from gplearn.genetic import SymbolicRegressor
import pandas as pd
import numpy as np

X = np.random.uniform(0,100,size=(100,3))
y = (X[:,0] - X[:,1])*X[:,2]

X_train , y_train = X[:80,:], y[:80]
X_test , y_test = X[80:,:], y[80:]

function_set = ['add', 'sub', 'mul', 'div', 'sqrt', 'log',
                'abs', 'neg', 'inv', 'max', 'min', 'sin', 'cos', 'tan']

est_gp = SymbolicRegressor(population_size=5000,
                           generations=20, stopping_criteria=0.01,
                           function_set=function_set,
                           p_crossover=0.7, p_subtree_mutation=0.1,
                           p_hoist_mutation=0.05, p_point_mutation=0.1,
                           max_samples=0.9, verbose=1,
                           parsimony_coefficient=0.01, random_state=0)

est_gp.fit(X_train, y_train)

Hi, very glad you like it!

It wouldn't be too hard to implement.
The _dispatch method in wide_format.py is the main entry point for the mutually recursive routines.
It already contains a nesting counter.
If you skip the body of it when the nesting depth is reached you're mostly done.
You may want to provide an indication that something was left out though.

However, depending on your needs, you might be better off using references to cap nesting.
Using separate files that refer to each other using $ref and $$target you don't need deep nesting but instead have parts of your schema hyperlinked.

Excerpt from one of my own schemas:

File **product.json** contains:
```
{
    "id": "<url referencing this file, required to make $ref use relative addressing>",
    "type": "object",
    "properties": {
        "startdate": {"$ref": "../shared/types.json/#date"},
        "enddate": {"$ref": "../shared/types.json/#date"},
        "supplier_startdate": {"$ref": "../shared/types.json/#nulleddate"},
        "supplier_enddate": {"$ref": "../shared/types.json/#nulleddate"}
    }
}
```

and **types.json** contains:
```
{
    "date": {
        "title": "Date",
        "$$target": ["#/date", "../shared/types.json/#date"],
        "description": "Date in international format YYYY-MM-DD",
        "type": "string",
        "pattern": "^[1-9][0-9]{3}-[01][0-9]-[0-3][0-9]$"
    },
    "nulleddate": {
        "title": "Optional date",
        "$$target": "../shared/types.json/#nulleddate",
        "description": "Optional date in international format YYYY-MM-DD",
        "oneOf": [
            {"$ref": "#/date"},
            {"type": "null"}
        ]
    }
}
```

I display product.json in a page detailing the product API and have a second page called "Types"
in which types.json is displayed.

Note the use of "$$target". Its contents should match exactly the corresponding $ref.
And since "date" is referenced from two different locations with two different "$ref" strings its
$$target is an array enumerating all those refs.

Hopes this helps to solve you issue.
If you still want to cap the recursion then by all means do and create a pull request for it.

regards,
Leo

I was surprised when the presence of `__pycache__` caused a conflict error from serverless-wsgi, when `__pycache__` was not going to be bundled anyway.

Now: python3.2 [changed](https://stackoverflow.com/questions/18426739/why-is-pyc-file-created-sometimes-in-same-directory-and-sometimes-in-pycache) from having `.pyc` files adjacent to the `.py` files, to having a `__pycache__` directory.

I wondered why a requirements bundle would ever have a *top-level* `__pycache__` directory.  The answer is because I'm using xmltodict, and this plops `xmltodict.py` at the top level, rather than using a package (`xmltodict/__init__.py`):

~~~
$ unzip -v .serverless/xxxxx.zip  | grep " __pycache__"
   13913  Defl:N     6989  50% 1980-01-01 00:00 81ca3cca  __pycache__/xmltodict.cpython-35.pyc
~~~

If you needed to upload your own `__pycache__` directory, *and* also the requirements has a top-level `__pycache__` directory, that's a pain.  serverless would have to merge the contents of the two directories.

> The bytecode files are cross-platform compatible, so they shouldn't be rebuilt on the server-side AFAIK

That's good.  Is that true even across python versions - in particular if I create `.pyc` files on python 3.5 under Ubuntu 16.04, they are usable on python 3.6 under Lambda?  If so then I don't really mind that they're there.

Omitting them would make the zip file a tiny bit smaller, which might help if you're getting near the 50MB limit, but is a minor optimisation.  Adding a few milliseconds to the first time a lamdba starts, I don't really care about.

So I should've mentioned these neopixels are very fussy on two levels: 

(a) they have to be driven at 800KHz and precise timing is crucial. I have one of the 8x4 Unicorn pHATs and that _seems_ to be fairly reliable. @fullphat has tried it with an 8x8 Unicorn HAT and things seem to go a bit harywire IIRC - see the discourse in issue #72. Search the internet, but the basic problem is that the RPi linux kernel can't emit timing signals in a guaranteed manner to support large chains of these devices.

(b) The voltage from the PWM pin needs level shifting from 3V3 to 5V. The Unicorn pHAT has some circuitry built in to do this, but I'm guessing the board you bought doesn't. See https://learn.adafruit.com/neopixels-on-raspberry-pi/wiring for details how to do this.

There is a new emerging "superled" called [APA102C](https://cpldcpu.com/2014/08/27/apa102/) which is driven by SPI, so interfacing would be a whole lot simpler.

`UnicodeDecodeError` happens because Python2 wants to convert a `unicode` object to a `str`. The patch changed logging on Python2 so both the log message and log arguments are `str`.

So either your test didn't happen on an exchangelib containing the patch, or we still have unicode arguments somewhere that are converted implicitly.

It's my understanding that logging in Python2 should be done with `str` objects, not `unicode`. Your PR goes against that.

First, virtualbox is really slow in general. So it's probably worth using VMware or running it some other way if possible. I wouldn't use virtualbox for anything intensive. That's probably the first adjustment to make.

Second, if you compiled dlib yourself, make sure it has SSE4 and AVX enabled. Those are features on newer CPUs (2011+) that speed up the processing a lot. Unfortunately I don't think the Sapphire 290 GPUs will be helpful. Most machine learning libraries are built on CUDA and cuDNN which is an nvidia product and only runs on nvidia GPUs. AMD GPUs aren't commonly used for machine learning right now, so they are not supported by any of the popular libraries.

Finally, the most important thing about image size is the actual resolution of the image, not the file size. You'll get way faster performance if you scale down your images before running face recognition on them. For example, scaling an image down to 640x480 would result in way faster performance than trying to process a 5MP picture right out of a cell phone camera. So try scaling down your images first. The trade-off is that the smaller you scale down the images, the less likely it is to find and recognize smaller faces in the image.

The built-in face_recognition command-line script tries to scale down your images to 1600 pixels wide here:

https://github.com/ageitgey/face_recognition/blob/master/face_recognition/cli.py#L36-L37

If you are using that script, you could try changing 1600 to 640 there and seeing how much faster it got.

I can't answer to all of your questions but serge will do it for me.

1) How `pythran` can survive among many other compilers?
I think we have 3 mains asset for this.
- good speed up which is what is required most of the time when someone is looking for a python compiler
- backward compatibility. So that users can test Pythran almost for free (it is not free, they have to install pythran)
- Many features. We support an increasing and important part of the Python language. Many others compiler are really specifics and can't compile many code we can find in stackoverflow.

I really think we should focus on it.

2) how `pythran` can support calling `c++` class like cython (great thing in cython)

Pulling this locally, it is quite unstable. I think a major issue is in the calculations of `p_0` and `p_1`. Consider this pseudo-code / pseudo-math

```
 negative_log_likelihood_values = (-np.log(p_0 * p_1))
                                                    = -np.log(p_0)  - np.log(p_1)
```
`-np.log(p_0)` is the logarithm of a ratio of gammas, so it can be expanded to sums and subtractions of the logarithm of gammas. There exists a numerically stable `gammaln` in scipy (I use it in the other fitters. 

Similarly, `-np.log(p_1)` is currently the product of exponentials. This is unstable for numbers far away from 0, so I would suggest expanding it into a summation.

>>> b = [{'a': 1}, {'a': 2}, {'a': 3}]
>>> print jsonpatch.make_patch(a, b)
[{"path": "/1/a", "value": 2, "op": "replace"}, {"path": "/2", "value": {"a": 3}, "op": "add"}]
```

If intend is to use this for human operators to show the differences between two documents, it is absolute need to get this fixed. Especially for very long lists or more complex list entries, it is essential doing a deepdiff to reduce the output to a minimum.

Example:
```
>>> a = [{'a': 1}, {'a': 3}, {'a': 4}, {'a': 5}, {'a': 6}, {'a': 7}, {'a': 8}, {'a': 9}, {'a': 10}]
>>> b = [{'a': 1}, {'a': 2}, {'a': 3}, {'a': 4}, {'a': 5}, {'a': 6}, {'a': 7}, {'a': 8}, {'a': 9}, {'a': 10}]
>>> print jsonpatch.make_patch(a, b)
[{"path": "/1/a", "value": 2, "op": "replace"}, {"path": "/2/a", "value": 3, "op": "replace"}, {"path": "/3/a", "value": 4, "op": "replace"}, {"path": "/4/a", "value": 5, "op": "replace"}, {"path": "/5/a", "value": 6, "op": "replace"}, {"path": "/6/a", "value": 7, "op": "replace"}, {"path": "/7/a", "value": 8, "op": "replace"}, {"path": "/8/a", "value": 9, "op": "replace"}, {"path": "/9", "value": {"a": 10}, "op": "add"}]
```

We've just inserted the 2nd item to the list, so output should just be:
```
[{"path": "/1", "value": {"a": 2}, "op": "add"}]
```

The standard errors are for each model parameter, and represents the standard deviation if you assume that the model is the correct form for each of the model parameters (that assumption includes that the break point locations are correct).

I still need to investigate an approach of a piecewise linear standard deviation... but in the meantime you can calculated the unbiased prediction variance and use it to generate your 95% confidence interval of your model.

I include a function to calculate the sum of squares of the residuals, which is the hard part.

Then all you need to do is calculate the unbiased prediction variance. It's usually easier though to work with standard deviations instead. 

```
# calculate the sum of the square of the residuals
ssr = my_pwlf.fit_with_breaks(my_pwlf.fit_breaks)

# calculate the unbiased standard deviation
sigma = np.sqrt(ssr / (my_pwlf.n_data - my_pwlf.n_parameters))
# sigma can be used as a prediction variance

# plot the results
plt.figure()
plt.plot(x, y, 'o')
plt.plot(xHat, yHat, '-')
plt.fill_between(xHat, yHat - (sigma*1.96), yHat + (sigma*1.96), alpha=0.1,
                 color="r")
plt.show()
```

Since i looked that up just now ill just document the issue:
With the current implementation + django up to and including 1.7.11, this only works if the value is an integer (or a single letter).
Since this is a CharField the default is passed through force_text before it reaches CheckboxSelectMultiple.render() while list is not a protected type.
See https://github.com/django/django/blob/1.7.11/django/db/models/fields/__init__.py#L720
vs https://github.com/django/django/blob/1.8/django/db/models/fields/__init__.py#L795

You COULD work around this by passing initial to the FormField instead of relying on default on the ModelField or using a callable as your default but since 1.7 is not supported since December 2015 an update to 1.8 LTS would be advisable.

Problem:

terminal one opens uWSGI server process which puts tasks into Redis,
terminal two opens Celery worker process which consumes task

The Redis instance is created in terminal one, and will terminate when terminal one closes, regardless of whether the process in terminal two is still polling for tasks every 2 seconds.

This is not a terribly huge problem, and while in a perfect world the Redis() instance might be aware that a client is polling and avoid tearing down the Redis instance, it's probably satisfactory for developers to understand this Redis instance is more ephermal/temperamental.

If you're seeing that error in the executor logs, then the issue is that the `HADOOP_HDFS_HOME` is not set on that node.  So, you can either ensure the nodes have the proper env vars, or you can try using `-conf spark.executorEnv.HADOOP_HDFS_HOME=/usr/hdp/current/hadoop-hdfs-client`.  Of course, this assumes that this path is correct across all of your executors.
@shaygeller Trying add `--conf spark.executorEnv.CLASSPATH="$($HADOOP_HOME/bin/hadoop classpath --glob):${CLASSPATH}"` to spark-submit.

FYI, I've submitted #37 which tries to detect the cause of the `KeyError` problem.  Note that this is really just a symptom of incorrect/invalid reservation.  In the current design, we assume that a Spark executor equates to a TF Node.  However, due to Spark's pool of Python workers (even for single-core configs), there is still the possibility that the same executor might `reserve` two different TF nodes of the cluster_spec.  And since we tie each TFNode to a TFManager via a unique (host, ppid) identifier (where ppid is the pid of the executor's JVM that spawned the Python worker), this can lead to incorrect behavior.

@Ruoyu-Qian if you notice, the difference is not just the path of the installation but the package itself. Your system has ``libBLAS.dylib``, whereas you need ``libRblas.0.dylib``

My guess is the R installation on your MacOS was not right because the latest R package when installed on Mac automatically configures ``openblas`` for better performance with array operations.

You could try linking your existing ``openblas`` to the R installation as follows -
```
cd /Library/Frameworks/R.framework/Versions/3.5/Resources/lib/
ln -sf  /System/Library/Frameworks/Accelerate.framework/Versions/A/Frameworks/vecLib.framework/Versions/A/libBLAS.dylib libRblas.dylib
```

Be careful here, as unrestrictedSearchResults remove three checks:
1. language
2. expiration
3. security

I'm not a fan of disabling all, just to get content in all languages.
1. use `.find(Language="")`
2. use `.find(show_inactive=True)`

The security check currently cannot be bypassed using a flag, but this can be done by using the context manager.

If this doesn't appeal to anyone, then I'd rather see `find_unrestricted` because it signals the dangers way better.

An implementation could be, and please try this in a client project:
```python
import copy
def find_unrestricted(*args, **kwargs):
    kwargs = copy.copy(kwargs)
    kwargs.update({'Language': '', 'show_inactive'=True}
    return find(*args, **kwargs
```

See stackoverflow.

> As long as I can load assimp output in Three.js, I think there's value to including the extension.

But that's just it: you can no longer load current assimp glTF2 output in the current version (r87) of three.js. They may load for you, because your version of Three.js loads the incorrect implementation of KHR_materials_common, but not for others.

I believe your `KHR_materials_common` implementation "targeted" an early (r85 or earlier) release of three.js, where it was using glTF 1.0's `KHR_materials_common spec`. Compare:

-  [r85's implementation of KHR_materials_common](https://github.com/mrdoob/three.js/blob/r85/examples/js/loaders/GLTF2Loader.js);
   - note storing the values in a `values` object, and the material property keys being `'diffuse', 'specular', 'shininess'
- compared to [the next revision's (r86) implementation]
(https://github.com/mrdoob/three.js/blob/r86/examples/js/loaders/GLTF2Loader.js#L391).
   - note the values being store not in a `values` object, and the material property keys being `diffuseFactor`, `diffuseTexture`, `specularFactor`, `specularTexture`, and `shininessFactor`

And even if we updated the implementation (if a work-in-progress spec could be agreed upon): that spec is bound to change in the future, again breaking exported models.

It's not wise to:

1. implement an unfinished, unratified spec; and,
1. use three.js (one importer, which itself has implemented incorrect versions of the spec) as the guide for how to implement it.

You're arguing for keeping something so that your pipeline will work, even if it's objectively incorrect.

> What value does removing it have?

The value is that it won't produce invalid glTF files that will be obsolete in the future (and are already obsolete)

> [...] I don't see any practical reason not to at least try to keep up with the WIP spec.

One reason is because it's generating objectively invalid glTF files. Whether it's the current assimp output (coded against glTF 1.0 KHR_materials_common), or models generated with the format from KhronosGroup/glTF#947, or the the format from KhronosGroup/glTF#965, or the spec from [KHR_materials_cmnBlinnPhong](https://github.com/UX3D-nopper/glTF/tree/master_lights_blinnphong/extensions/Khronos/KHR_materials_cmnBlinnPhong). They can, and likely will all change or go away.

pbrMetallicRoughness (part of core spec) and pbrSpecularGlossiness (official, ratified extension) are at least set in stone and will continue to work as long as there are loaders for glTF2.

>> assimp currently doesn't export KHR_materials_common properly against any of the WIP specs.

> I can certainly believe that, but I think the solution is to fix the implementation.

As in my first paragraph: which work-in-progress spec do you target against, though?

Looks to be an known issue with Homebrew'd python: https://github.com/Homebrew/brew/blob/master/docs/Homebrew-and-Python.md#note-on-pip-install---user

Note that `shiv` is not using the `--user` command, but it does use `--target` which suffers the same fate.

You can see that plain old `pip install` exhibits the problem:

```
lcarvalh-mn1 ~ ❱❱❱ /usr/local/Cellar/python/3.6.3/bin/pip install --target temp_dir aws --no-deps
Collecting aws
  Using cached https://files.pythonhosted.org/packages/90/56/d088e68699e4f00881d957584e26854a88d0009864ef614c6f567807fc2f/aws-0.2.5.tar.gz
Skipping bdist_wheel for aws, due to binaries being disabled for it.
Installing collected packages: aws
  Running setup.py install for aws ... done
Exception:
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "/usr/local/Cellar/python3/3.6.3/Frameworks/Python.framework/Versions/3.6/lib/python3.6/site-packages/pip/basecommand.py", line 215, in main
    status = self.run(options, args)
  File "/usr/local/Cellar/python3/3.6.3/Frameworks/Python.framework/Versions/3.6/lib/python3.6/site-packages/pip/commands/install.py", line 350, in run
    isolated=options.isolated_mode,
  File "/usr/local/Cellar/python3/3.6.3/Frameworks/Python.framework/Versions/3.6/lib/python3.6/site-packages/pip/commands/install.py", line 436, in get_lib_location_guesses
    scheme = distutils_scheme('', *args, **kwargs)
  File "/usr/local/Cellar/python3/3.6.3/Frameworks/Python.framework/Versions/3.6/lib/python3.6/site-packages/pip/locations.py", line 153, in distutils_scheme
    i.finalize_options()
  File "/usr/local/Cellar/python3/3.6.3/Frameworks/Python.framework/Versions/3.6/lib/python3.6/site-packages/setuptools/command/install.py", line 38, in finalize_options
    orig.install.finalize_options(self)
  File "/usr/local/Cellar/python3/3.6.3/Frameworks/Python.framework/Versions/3.6/lib/python3.6/distutils/command/install.py", line 248, in finalize_options
    "must supply either home or prefix/exec-prefix -- not both")
distutils.errors.DistutilsOptionError: must supply either home or prefix/exec-prefix -- not both
```

I tried one of the workarounds I found while googling around and it seemed to fix the problem:

```
lcarvalh-mn1 ~ ❱❱❱ cat << EOF > ~/.pydistutils.cfg
> [install]
> prefix=
> EOF
lcarvalh-mn1 ~ ❱❱❱ /usr/local/Cellar/python/3.6.3/bin/pip3 install --target temp_dir aws --no-deps
Collecting aws
Installing collected packages: aws
Successfully installed aws-0.2.5
```

However, you might still run into issues because that package (`aws`) appears to only support Python2 :(
That said, I was able to build a pyz and run it after using the above `~/.pydistutils.cfg` trick:

```
lcarvalh-mn1 ~ ❱❱❱ shiv aws -c aws -p /usr/local/Cellar/python/3.6.3/bin/python3 -o blergh -q
lcarvalh-mn1 ~ ❱❱❱ ./aws
usage: aws [options] <command> <subcommand> [<subcommand> ...] [parameters]
To see help text, you can run:

  aws help
  aws <command> help
  aws <command> <subcommand> help
aws: error: the following arguments are required: command
```

Hello @jimymodi ! From the snippet you wrote, you're using properly the context manager with the first `with`. Unfortunately, in the second `tracer.trace('kafka.message.decode')` you're creating a `Span` without finishing it. To make it work the snippet should be something like:
```python
with tracer.trace('kafka.message', service='es-streaming-transporter', resource='Consumer') as span:
	span.set_tag("version", "1.0")
	span = tracer.trace('kafka.message.decode')
	# do something with `span`, or just use the `with` like the first line
	logging.info('decoding')
	span.finish()
```

All created spans must be finished otherwise the full trace will not be sent in the agent. Can you confirm if it's working? Thank you!

There's no rST required in my suggestion, it would be a Markdown extension syntax chosen from the current discussion on the commonmark spec around extensions. It's also something this module will adopt as soon as the extension spec is ratified. It wouldn't be a standard addition that other flavors/etc of markdown support, but ¯\_(ツ)_/¯ what is.

I do agree missing a table syntax would be an abrupt change for anyone more familiar with $Whatever Flavored Markdown. Perhaps table syntax could be another feature we preemptively adopt from the commonmark spec discussions as an experimental feature.

I also think most authors have varying degrees of Stockholm Syndrome when it comes to the deficiencies of authoring complex documents with markdown. Table syntax inside any markup language -- rST included -- is an utterly broken experience for authors. Docutils provides an infinitely better experience for managing large tables outside of the markup syntax. Short tables are of course better served by a native markup table syntax, though.

> any suggestions for what should raise/return the other functions?

I think it's fine if a function that creates files throws an `IOError` if (eg.) the filesystem is un-writeable, or a `ValueError` when passed something of the right type but wrong value. The problem with `discover_repository`'s `KeyError` is that a key error is not what happened. Documenting the common exceptions that your function can throw is helpful.

----

The other approach is to catch and wrap everything that comes out of your library in a library-specific exception, and not just `GitError` (because then you *lose* information, or have to inspect the inner exception) but a specific subclass of `GitError` for the particular way something went wrong. I think that's a lot of work for not much benefit, and if you decide to go the other way later you can always make your exception inherit from the thing it's replacing so that `FileSystemUnwriteableError(GitError, IOError)` is still catchable with `except IOError`.

> So it's possible to get around that by doing something like instead of a hash, baking the contents of Pipfile itself into the Pipfile.lock, in this way you'd end up with cleaner diff (although it doesn't solve the abstract problem of merge conflicts that can still happen and require a way to deal with them).

Using this approach, the main benefit is that a human is able to resolve the merge conflict pretty easily.

Okay, so [the Django docs actually do mention](https://docs.djangoproject.com/en/1.9/topics/migrations/#serializing-values) that nested classes can't be serialized.

The `MyModel.Color` enum from the `tests` package gets serialized wrongly to

```python
('color', enumfields.fields.EnumField(enum=tests.models.Color, max_length=1)),
```

Of course you could manually edit the `enum` field there, but that might be tedious.

On Python 3, one could use `__qualname__` to construct a better qualified path to the object:

```python
>>> MyModel.Color
<enum 'Color'>
>>> MyModel.Color.__qualname__
'MyModel.Color'
>>> MyModel.Color.__name__
'Color'
>>>
```

but that's something Django would have to do, not this package. So changing the tests and docs!

Here's a follow up:

After re-installing the anaconda distro I still had the above mentioned issue with the six package as well as not being able to import matplotlib.pyplot. My solution was to go through each package that I found in the traceback (including six, numpy, scipy, and PyQt4) and check whether python was sourcing the package from anaconda or python2.7/dist-tools. 

If the package was in dist-tools, I would navigate to the dist-tools dir and run 
```
sudo apt-get remove package-name
sudo rm -rf package-name
```

That got my scikit-image library completely working.

I'm still not sure what broke it in the first place, but it seemed to be more of a matplotlib dependency issue.

Also thanks @blink1073  for the suggestion to use conda for my virtual environments. That makes my life much easier.

So before to go further, I would like some advice regarding:

- the unit tests;
- the performance of the integral computation.

### Unit tests

For the moment, I did not implement unit tests per se. I wanted to know what would be the best strategy.
I add in mind to generate mask using the visualization function and check if it corresponds to what is expected. We can also give `np.ones` input which should give a known output for the different type of features. However, it does not allow to check all possibilities, so WDYT?

### Performances

I wrote the code such that it fits with the `skimage.transform.integrate` function.
However, I saw that there is no cython version of the code and I would think that the benefit of such Haar-like feature is also the speed for which the feature can be computed (if I remember well my master course, it was supposed to solve some real-time problems at testing). I would agree that the code is quite user-friendly and readable but at the cost of speed limitation.

Therefore, is it wise to reuse the `integrate` function or implement a pure C (I mean Cython) version?

I'll bat for this. It is useful in cases where you want to check if an attribute/dimension of has a dependency on previous values of the attribute.(Something similar to [time-series stationarity test ](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dickey%E2%80%93Fuller_test) ). 
The point is if there's a fractal dimension then we know that this series of values have some dependency on previous values and will change depending on what the initial conditions were. 

It can help us identify, strange attractors / local optima.

Thanks for answer. Please note, I'm not arguing against adding support for messaging signing. Also, I do recognise that the most easy implementation is to use what Celery already have (and it means we  have less code to maintain). 

My concern is purely related to trade off between complexity and security. Security is also a matter of simplicity and easily manageable solutions. If I understand correct, you would need one private/public key per node, and share all the public keys between the machines to have any benefit over a solution based on a shared secret (which doesn't have to be the one in ``SECRET_KEY``)? 
In the end, I'm concerned that adding something which is hard to manage in a production system won't be used, and thus have no effect, whereas if we enable message signing by default using a shared secret we already have, then it adds security by default. 

**Comments on specific points:**

> RabbitMQ is not the standard way proposing in our docs. We use Redis, for different purposes, without write protection.

For all current production system RabbitMQ is the defacto standard. The fact that only Redis is mentioned in the docs is a bug, and an artefact of that we keep dev setups as simple as possible. Also, our docs basically lacks any kind of production setup guide (the little that is here http://invenio.readthedocs.org/en/latest/getting-started/overlay.html was never running on an actual production system)

> The size of our codebase is so enormous that I would even bet that it contains at least one critical security bug.

Yes, I'm quite sure about that as well, and I don't think it's only 1. Please do go hunt for them. A big reason here I believe is the complexity fo the code base, which is being addressed by our road map for Invenio 3.0

> Our "secret key" is NOT a state-of-the-art key. It is just a secret. If exposed, it is possible to use it for different API and entry points. 

Isn't the same true for the private key which could get expose as easily as our secret key? If our secret key is weak then please ticketize so we can fix it.

> We do not even have a good rejection mechanism which tells us which effects a change of the secret will have.

Agree. Will you ticketize so that we can start working on it? Somekind of deprecation mechanism that would allow us to easily change our secret key on a regular basis would be great. 

> Using the same key for different purposes is a bad idea.

For my understanding, do you have some examples why it would be bad in this case?

> Arguing that we already have a firewall does not help. Should you somehow get write access to the network (in case the firewall fails) you can execute every possible command our workers (remote code execution).

I see this as an argument against using Redis as the broker. RabbitMQ requires authentication (which should naturally happen over an encrypted connection).

> Adding additional layers of security is good practice and is expected by project from our size.

Yes, and I'm also not arguing against adding message signing or the extra security. My concern is tradeoffs between two different solutions to adding extra security.

> A last word on firewalls: it might protect one specific way to enter your systems (like a wall), but it won't protect you from other (more creative) ways, e.g. when other systems behind it have vulnerabilities.

Naturally not. Gaining access to any of the nodes where the private key is stored, would also allow you to do remote code execution.

First a question: If this design is accepted, would it replace the existing code or be an alternative implementation, giving to overlay developers the possibility to choose one or the other?

And 3 different notes:

1/ It seems to me that this proposal describes how the permissions are represented in the record, but not how they would be checked in Invenio-access's code.

We have a requirement for B2Share which will probably be overkill for most other Invenio services but maybe it can work with the design suggested by @egabancho.
In B2Share some **actions** on the REST API can require one or more permissions depending.

Example:
We have a `publication status` field in our deposits. To publish a record the user changes this field's value from `draft` or `submitted` to `published`. This will trigger the creation of the final record.
Thus a PATCH action can either:
- modify the deposit's metadata
- change the state of the deposit.
- do both
And the tricky part is that the permissions are not the same for changing the state of the deposit and to modify the its metadata. A user can have one and not the other, none or both.


2/ @aw-bib I see one problem if the users start modifying permissions: access rights are given for specific reasons. Sometime multiple reasons give the same access right (example: user is owner and is in a specific group of users). By manipulating permissions instead of the original reasons, the result can be extremely difficult to understand or change programmatically (ex: during migrations).


3/ Another thing which could be useful, but is more a nice to have, is to somehow be able to recompute *why* a given user has a given permission.
[Here is an example of how salesforce UI looks like when explaining permissions.](http://www.salesforcehacker.com/2013/09/build-your-own-custom-sharing-button.html)

I think overall there were several attempts to be able to write SPARQL queries in a more pythonic way already, but never really followed through and made it "feature complete", so I'd say contribs always welcome... 

Some thoughts:

I'd probably still user longer names (users can shorten them via `import ... as ...` if they really want to, but it will reduce the amount of "WTFs" for newcomers a lot) and realize this with a QueryBuilder class that supports chaining:

```python
from rdflib import *
from rdflib.query_builder import QueryBuilder, FILTER, SUM
Var = Variable

tim = URIRef("http://www.w3.org/People/Berners-Lee/card#i")
qb = QueryBuilder().SELECT(
    middle_aged_friend=Var('s'),
    some_sum=SUM(VAR('age')),
).WHERE(
    (tim, FOAF.knows, Var('s')),
    (tim, FOAF.age, Var('age')),
    FILTER(Literal(35) < Var('age')),  # this will probably be the most challenging
    FILTER(Var('age') < Literal(65)),
).LIMIT(10)
```

I'd recommend not to use `v.X` but `Var['X']` or `Var('X')`, as the `.` notation at some point always causes people to run into collisions with method names of `Variable`. I know that pycharm will probably auto-complete 

Also, `filter` is a python keyword, one could hackishly use `FILTER`, but it might confuse people :-/

And last but not least, implementing all `FILTER` operators on rdflib terms like `Literal`, `BNode`, `URIRef` and `Variable` will for sure cause some huge headaches as some of the ops are already defined (in different ways). Maybe one could go for separate implementations of the terms for the query builder, but that also has a huge potential of confusing people.

Thinking about especially the last point, the SPARQL parder, algebra and [operators](https://github.com/RDFLib/rdflib/blob/master/rdflib/plugins/sparql/operators.py) come to mind... maybe parts of that can be re-used...

hi @rafaelcaricio,

I have added the "utf-8" parameter in the decoding function in all the tests. This has fixed all the test cases in python 2.x tests.

 But python 3.x  test cases are failing due to two errors
1) Assertion error assert 'b\'"DummyClass"\'\n' == '"DummyClass"\n'
2) json.decoder.JSONDecodeError: Expecting value: line 1 column 1 (char 0)

The decode function in Python3.x somehow is not stripping the prefix 'b' from the response. This is failing the 1) assertion and 2) json.loads (as it expects a bracket and not a character at the beginning)

A prefix of 'b' or 'B' is ignored in Python 2. Python 3 clearly represent byte string and they are still valid strings.

We could either not perform any encoding in python 3.x as utf-8 encoding is well handled in Python3 internally or strip the prefix b's off before assertion and json.loads.

I will continue to check further in details if it is some other reason for the problem.

**dwhjames**
**gustavo.grieco**, looking at the impl. of `ABI.make_function_arguments` it doesn’t look like serialization of something like `myFunc(address[2])` is handled correctly
or at least to make it do the right thing, one would have to call it as though it’s actually `myFunc(address,address)`

**gustavo.grieco**
Uhm.

**dwhjames**
and on the other side array support is also partial in `ABI._consume_type`, i.e., only `address[]` is handled?
the tests in https://github.com/trailofbits/manticore/pull/715
are only for `address[]`

**gustavo.grieco**
I created an issue to request the implementation of dynamic arguments and provide an small example contract using `address[]` as example. But the implementation should be easily extended to other types of arrays.
Tomorrow I check the serialization of `address[2]` and submit a bug report if necessary.

**dwhjames**
```python
from manticore.ethereum import ABI
spec1 = 'myFunc(uint256[2])'
calldata1 = ABI.make_function_call(spec1, [42, 1337])
ABI.parse(spec1, calldata1)
```

1. `calldata1` has a dynamic encoding of the array, not a static one
2. `ABI.parse` raises an error because it doesn’t know about arrays for `uint256` and tries to convert `256[2]` to an `int`

```python
from manticore.ethereum import ABI
spec2 = 'myFunc(address[2])'
calldata2 = ABI.make_function_call(spec1, 0x1, 0x2)
ABI.parse(spec2, calldata2)
```

1. by exploding the two-array and treating it as a two-param function `calldata2` is correct, according to the ABI spec
2. but `ABI.parse` raises a `NotImplementedError`
`ABI.make_function_call` has the ABI spec, but it delegates to `ABI.make_function_arguments`, which does not consult the spec, and instead dispatches off of the Python types, so in the current design there is no way to handle both static and dynamic arrays
`ABI._consume_type` is simply missing the cases to the handle any array type other than a dynamic array of addresses

---

So, with all that context, I thought I’d respond to your questions in the scope of `ABI.make_function_call` and `ABI.make_function_arguments`—the primary way I believe that most Manticore users would be interacting with the behavior of `ABI.serialize`.

Right now, the subset of Solidity types supported are approximated with Python types. `ABI.serialize` certainly not a surjective function, as not all Solidity types are supported, but it’s not really even a function in the mathematical sense, as a Python long is mapped into all sorts of types.

We can get away with long for (u)int8–256, address, and bytes32. And if one does the required left-shift, then a long will even suffice for types bytes1–31.

Static arrays, as I pointed out on the Slack channel run into a brick wall with the inference-by-Python-type approach. And the `function` type as another sort of compound type is also problematic.

So, my thoughts thus far:

1. I think there needs to be a `ABI.make_function_arguments_with_spec(spec, *args)` that `ABI.make_function_call` uses.
2. Having a Python long overloaded for all sorts of uses is rather convenient and succinct, but that approximation also causes problems when one wants precise control. That’s why I started introducing nested classes to enumerate each Solidity type (`StaticBytes`, `Address`, `ExternalFunction`, etc.). I can see how this becomes un-Pythonic, as my understanding of that philosophy is that there should be a canonical way of doing everything. And I may have gone overboard with the convenience constructors that would be accepting of non-canonical representations. Nonetheless, I think Manticore ultimately needs a representation of all Solidity types as new nominal types, rather than overloading built-in Python types.

pyproj is included with the matplotlib [Basemap](http://matplotlib.org/basemap/) toolkit.  This is a separate install from matplotlib.  Currently, Py-ART requires matplotlib to be installed but not Basemap.  Basemap is quite a large install (~180 MB) as it includes a number of large data files.  Therefore we would like to keep Basemap as an optional dependency, which means pyproj is an optional dependency. 

The rough plan is to implement one map projection directly in Py-ART (azimuth equidistant seems like the best choice) so that multi-radar gridding is possible without pyproj/Basemap as long as the user accepts this projection.  If Basemap/pyproj is available the user would be able to choose any number of map projections for the gridding.

I should not that it is possible to install [pyproj](https://github.com/jswhit/pyproj) independently of Basemap, but Py-ART does not support this yet.  prproj by itself is a much smaller install (~1-2 MB).

Is this what you are doing?

r = requests.get(url, auth=HTTPKerberosAuth("HTTP/poke.INTexample.org@INT.EXAMPLE.ORG), verify=False)

HTTP authentication is never connection-based, it's always transactional. That is, a single request is authed. This was a major part of why NTLM was a problem for HTTP: NTLM authenticated connections, rather than transactions.

With the additional fact that urllib3 will transparently reconnect when a HTTP connection has been lost (e.g. because it fell idle), I don't think you could implement 2 with any grace, even if it was a good idea.

That said, I'd be interested to see what, say, Firefox or curl do for mutual auth. @bagder, mind lending us your wisdom?

- `Key(..., namespace=...)`
- `Query(..., namespace=...)`

Thanks for reporting. This is actually deeper than it seems. The "correct" fix is for u to remove the (non-public) `google.streaming` stuff that this relies on and get a better chunking story (that doesn't rely on `httplib2`).

Your option 1 is similar to what we do in Java as well. Note that when you retry you will have to pass back the resume_token, so the retry function could possible take a resume token as an argument.

Also note this is more complex because we do not guarantee that all PartialResultSet would have a resume token. So if you yield a Row to the user but the PRS that contained that Row did not have a resume_token, you cannot retry safely. So what you will need to do is store PRS's in a buffer till you see one with resume_token. Only then yield them to the user. So essentially you guarantee that every time you yield something to the user it is at a resumable boundary.

Are you just looking to filter one record? You could do that overriding `get_queryset`. 

I have a `ParentDatatableView` that I use, it populates an `object` with the parent data and it has a `datatable` as detail.

This is the code I use

```
class ParentObjectMixin(ExtraContextMixin):
    parent_model = None
    context_object_name = 'object'
    object = None
    pk_url_kwarg = 'pk'
    slug_field = None
    slug_url_kwarg = 'slug'
    parent_queryset = None

    def get_context_data(self, **kwargs):
        context = super(ParentObjectMixin, self).get_context_data(**kwargs)
        obj = self.get_parent_object()
        context[self.context_object_name] = obj
        return context

    def get_slug_field(self):
        """
        Get the name of a slug field to be used to look up by slug.
        """
        return self.slug_field

    def get_parent_object(self, parent_queryset=None):
        """
        Returns the object the view is displaying.
        By default this requires `self.parent_queryset` and a `pk` or `slug` argument
        in the URLconf, but subclasses can override this to return any object.
        """
        # Use a custom parent_queryset if provided; this is required for subclasses
        # like DateDetailView
        if parent_queryset is None:
            parent_queryset = self.get_parent_queryset()
        # Next, try looking up by primary key.
        pk = self.kwargs.get(self.pk_url_kwarg)
        slug = self.kwargs.get(self.slug_url_kwarg)
        if pk is not None:
            parent_queryset = parent_queryset.filter(pk=pk)
        # Next, try looking up by slug.
        if slug is not None and (pk is None or self.query_pk_and_slug):
            slug_field = self.get_slug_field()
            parent_queryset = parent_queryset.filter(**{slug_field: slug})
        # If none of those are defined, it's an error.
        if pk is None and slug is None:
            raise AttributeError("Generic detail view %s must be called with "
                                 "either an object pk or a slug."
                                 % self.__class__.__name__)
        try:
            # Get the single item from the filtered parent_queryset
            obj = parent_queryset.get()
        except parent_queryset.model.DoesNotExist:
            raise Http404("No %(verbose_name)s found matching the query" %
                          {'verbose_name': parent_queryset.model._meta.verbose_name})
        return obj

    def get_parent_queryset(self):
        """
        Return the `parent_queryset` that will be used to look up the object.
        Note that this method is called by the default implementation of
        `get_object` and may not be called if `get_object` is overridden.
        """
        if self.parent_queryset is None:
            if self.parent_model:
                return self.parent_model._default_manager.all()
            else:
                raise ImproperlyConfigured(
                    "%(cls)s is missing a parent_queryset. Define "
                    "%(cls)s.model, %(cls)s.parent_queryset, or override "
                    "%(cls)s.get_parent_queryset()." % {
                        'cls': self.__class__.__name__
                    }
                )
        return self.parent_queryset.all()

class ParentDatatableView(ParentObjectMixin, DatatableMixin, ListView):
    template_name = 'core/datatable.html'
```

And to implement I use something like this:

```

class ListTrainingRecord(StaffuserRequiredMixin, ParentDatatableView):
    model = LegacyCourseTaken
    datatable_class = LegacyTrainingRecordTable
    title = "Training Record"
    parent_model = Employee
    slug_url_kwarg = 'username'
    slug_field = 'user_name'

    def get_queryset(self):
        qs = super(ListTrainingRecord, self).get_queryset()
        # queryset is for the datatable data, get the kwarg to filter
        pk = self.kwargs.get(self.pk_url_kwarg)
        employee = get_object_or_404(Employee, user_name=self.kwargs.get(self.slug_url_kwarg))
        return qs.filter(employee=employee)
```

Looking at the parameters you were using for training I can spot two differences to the ones used in the experiments for the paper (see https://github.com/awslabs/sockeye/blob/arxiv_1217/arxiv/code/transformer/sockeye/train-transformer.sh). Namely:
 * `--clip-gradient=-1`
 * `--transformer-dropout-relu`

Now these of course won't have any influence on the number of batches, but they might affect your final BLEU score.
How did you run BLEU evaluation? Did you run both tensor2tensor and Sockeye through [ScareBLEU](https://github.com/awslabs/sockeye/blob/master/contrib/sacrebleu/sacrebleu.py)?

With regards to the updates per epoch: the training data has around 160 million words. With a batch size of 4096 this gives around 39,000 batches. If you only see 6000 updates per epoch in tensor2tensor you are probably using a batch size larger than 4096 words per batch. With the 160 million source words the batch size most probably be something like 26k words. Could you share your tensor2tensor configuration?

Also could you paste your sockeye training log for reference?

@tomercagan thanks for confirming upgrading pygments fixes the issue.

I can duplicate the issue with these steps:

* Have `Pygments` previously installed, say version `1.6`
* Install `aws-shell`, which does not have a `pygments` requirement explicitly set in `setup.py`

`aws-shell` has a requirement on `prompt-toolkit` which just specifies a `pygments` dependency with no version, so `Pygments 1.6` is not upgraded and we see the startup issue.

Short term solution is probably to explicitly add the `Pygments>=2.1.3` dependency to `aws-shell`.

As for the medium term, note the latest `prompt-toolkit 0.60` has actually removed the `Pygments` dependency entirely: https://github.com/jonathanslenders/python-prompt-toolkit/commit/401cbbb96685431abe8ecee033740c9a180badf0#diff-2eeaed663bd0d25b7e608891384b7298.

We might want to get https://github.com/awslabs/aws-shell/pull/109 wrapped up first then move to `0.60`.

You can reference stage variables in the api integration uri:
```yaml
# main template
Resources:
  Api:
    Type: AWS::Serverless::Api
    Properties:
      DefinitionUri: api.yml
      Variables:
        MyFn: !Ref MyFn
  MyFn:
    Type: AWS::Serverless::Function
    Properties:
      Events:
        Api:
          Type: Api
          Properties:
            Path: /my-fn
            Method: GET
            RestApiId: !Ref Api
```

```yaml
# swagger
paths:
  /my-fn:
    get:
      x-amazon-apigateway-integration:
        httpMethod: POST
        type: aws_proxy
        uri: arn:aws:apigateway:us-east-1:lambda:path/2015-03-31/functions/arn:aws:lambda:us-east-1:00000000:function:${stageVariables.MyFn}/invocations
```
You still have to hardcode the region and account ID, but at least you don't have to know the fn arn ahead of time.
