@dslagle I am going to answer both your questions here.

For your first comment:
I don't completely understand your current setup. Are you trying to have only one Lambda Function that you can use for dev, test, and prod? That would mean all of these are controlled through the same CloudFormation stack?
If that is the case, I do not recommend you (or anyone) to use aliases for various environments. The biggest concern I have with this is that you are running the risk of impacting prod with a change to test or dev. You are creating a bigger blast radius in the event something goes wrong with a deploy. My other concern would be around security of your functions. You may have to add credentials or policies specific to dev or test which will or could be replicated in prod. My suggestion is to split out dev, test, and prod into separate CloudFormation stacks so that each environments in isolated for each other. You then only have to manage the one CloudFormation template and can deploy it through you CI/CD system at an environment level. You will still only be managing one Lambda function (through SAM) but this setup will reduce blast radius for deployments and isolate your different environment's functions and resources. 

If that is not what you are attempting to do, can you explain your setup further or what you are trying to achieve?

For your second comment:
Alias does support intrinsics but we limited it only to Ref currently. 

The team knows that documentation and intrinsic support is an area for improvement. We are working towards this improvement but will take some time to get out. Right now, I do not have a good way to surface this information to you or the community. I would like us to do this the right way and get better documentation up. We may do a quick update to surface this information but will depend on how far out the documentation update is. I know this isn't a great answer but hopefully this pain will go away soon enough.

Ok, that's exactly the `play_local_files` example from this repository.
The error occurs in these two lines:
```python
number_in_queue = zone.add_uri_to_queue(netpath)    
zone.play_from_queue(number_in_queue)
```
I suspect that `add_uri_to_queue` returns a 1-based index, while `play_from_queue()` expects a 0-based index. This means that if the item is enqueued as the last item, `number_in_queue` will not be a valid 0-based index, leading to the "Illegal seek target" UPnP error.

def __hash__(self):
    variable_hashes = [hash(variable) for variable in self.variables]
    sorted_var_hashes = sorted(variable_hashes)
    phi = self.copy()
    for axis in range(phi.values.ndim):
        exchange_index = variable_hashes.index(sorted_var_hashes[axis])
        variable_hashes[axis], variable_hashes[exchange_index] = (variable_hashes[exchange_index], variable_hashes[axis])
        phi.cardinality[axis], phi.cardinality[exchange_index] = (phi.cardinality[exchange_index],
                                                                  phi.cardinality[axis])
        phi.values = phi.values.swapaxes(axis, exchange_index)
    return hash("".join(map(str, sorted_var_hashes)) + str(phi.values) + str(phi.cardinality))
```

PyDAQmx was developed in the community before NI developed an official Python API.  While PyDAQmx provided a path for Python users to access NI Data Acquisition for several years, we felt we could add value by providing a more pythonic api with more complete documentation, optional parameters and a focus on ease of use.  There are a few notable differences:

- nidaqmx has more significant API documentation for attributes and methods
- nidaqmx methods have reasonable default values for many parameters which are optional
- nidaqmx is static rather than dynamically generated at runtime allowing easier inspection of the API
- nidaqmx is the official National Instruments supported API

In many respects PyDAQmx has been a pretty shallow wrapper over the NI-DAQmx C API.  nidaqmx is focused on being more pythonic and providing a more natural experience and better ade support for Python developers.

There are numerous differences between Deis and Flynn, Tsuru, Paz, Solum, Dokku, Dokku-Alt, Galaxy, and other Heroku like PaaS implementations. There are numerous other PaaS implementations that aren't git push centric, like Stratos (WSO2), Cloud Foundry, OpenShift, Stakato, Cloudify, but even those are easily hooked into a git repo to trigger after a git push finishes. The real challenge in any PaaS is release versioning, orchestration of rolls, container scheduling, and ancillary services.

Flynn doesn't use CoreOS, it prefers Ubuntu, though there is "no actual package dependency on it". 

Flynn has a random scheduler, Deis inherits the "least number of units" scheduler presently in Fleet.

Flynn has a postgres database layer using a master/slave failover technique modeled after Joyent SDC Manatee, with no direct backups with wal-e yet.

Deis prefers to run one postgres master (deis-database) and use wal-e and s3 compatible ceph radosgw (deis-store-gateway) for backups and restores.

Deis implements everything as Fleet units which run simple scripts or docker containers with the complexity embedded within. This makes Deis feel very modular (because it is!) 

For CAP, Deis inherits Fleet and etcd for GOSSIP/RAFT by using CoreOS. Many other opensource projects use etcd as well (kubernetes, etc). Flynn has its own discoverd RAFT implementation.

You _can_ submit "services" in flynn via some pretty interesting JSON posts, but it's not really intuitive yet. CoreOS fleet units model well in cloud-init yaml files, and can be installed alongside Deis. Here are some of mine: https://github.com/ianblenke/coreos-vagrant-kitchen-sink/tree/master/tested

This is my limit of exposure to Flynn at the moment. It does seem a bit young, but promising. Many other OpenSource projects have borrowed heavily from Flynn over the past year. It has a bit of maturing to go through operationally, but I wouldn't count it out (continue to keep an eye on it).

There are many other PaaS options out there. I keep coming back to Deis, mostly because I like CoreOS cloud-init fleet units, and I can wrap my brain around how the fleet units that comprise Deis work together.

Honestly, it feels a bit disingenuous to compare Deis to Flynn when there are so many other PaaS implementations to compare it to as well. Gaining operational experience with these tools is something we're all in the middle of at the moment. It probably isn't fair to compare them yet, as most of them are still very young in their lifecycles.

@krancour I like your idea of forcing users to use version control with `rigger` out of the box. I'm in agreement with the init -> config -> provision model. However, I want to be careful that we don't add too many dependencies for rigger itself (or it can self manage those dependencies). I have essentially two targets of people:

- people who just want to get Deis up and running with the least amount of friction (this could even be us while demo-ing Deis somewhere... and fewer moving parts is better here)
- people who care more about how easy the cluster will be to manage over time (this is definitely the case for the background that you and lots of current Deis community members come from)

I think there's a way to establish a happy medium by figuring out what the user is after, but early in the "configure" process (leaning toward doing your suggested `init` operation automatically if/when it makes sense).

For the idea of having `rigger` create vpcs, RDS databases, and S3 buckets... those actual operations seem like something CloudFormation should do (or Terraform) and could be done during the provision step. Why would we want to inject a prereq step before actual Deis provisioning + deployment? Why can't the single provision step do [everything](http://www.zombo.com/)?

Code LGTM.

> Is possible to just run `kubeletet create -f`. Is possible to run that after the k8s cluster is created

Well, yes and no. To explain how this works (for others who may not know), the salt bootstrap process installs the manifest to [/etc/kubernetes/manifests/fluentd-es.yaml](https://github.com/kubernetes/kubernetes/blob/master/cluster/saltbase/salt/fluentd-es/init.sls#L1-L3) on every node, which the kubelet will then read on boot and start that pod on every node that has that manifest file on-disk. We cannot run `kubelet create -f` on a node that we do not have SSH access to, such as the case with users who may want to run Deis on [Google Container Engine](https://cloud.google.com/container-engine/). You only get access to the kubectl API so that's what we're restricted to. I recall somewhere on the k8s issue list that someone has found a workaround using port affinity, so that will likely be the workaround for Deis agents.

@csoja and @kalefranz I get it.  The answer for people who want things done this way is going to be something like:

1. Mirror `rpy2` from PyPI into Anaconda Repository
2. Install R however you like onto your system(s).
3. Install the build chain on each system.
4. `pip install rpy2` pulling the package from Anaconda Repository

Or alternate approach from step 3 onwards:

3. Install the build chain onto one representative system
4. `conda skeleton pypi rpy2` (pointing it to Anaconda Repository PyPI mirror)
5. `conda build rpy2`
6. `conda upload /path/to/package`
7. On all systems that require it, `conda install -c localr rpy2

There is no reason to treat `pkgs/urls.txt` special (it wasn't treated special before either).  The only problem was that `pkgs_dir` wasn't global.  I've added `global pkgs_dir` to my original PR https://github.com/conda/conda/pull/2756 and this is also working now.

That said: I have an idea for another approach to the `generate_feature_metric` method; @mbargull you may want to try this. The idea is this. Right now the two feature optimization passes are:

1. Minimize the number of active features activated.
2. Maximize the number of packages installed that contain an active feature.

The problem has been with step 2 there, and how it has been implemented. Indeed, as I've stated it here, it's quite obvious why it's causing the problems @SylvainCorlay is seeing now. But I think a better metric is this:

2. Minimize the number of packages installed with _feature mismatch_. That is: the package _has_ a featured alternative, but it was not installed for other reasons (say, dependency conflicts).

Here is the code I've written to generate this optimization metric. @mbargull it doesn't break the test I created for PR #6766. And I _believe_ this will resolve the behavior we're seeing here, because it's not doing anything to _favor_ the installation of a package you didn't ask for.

`conda list --explicit` lists all installed conda packages with URL. This is not an environment file. 
I see that you're using the command `conda env create --name testingenv -f spec-file.txt` to create an environment, but this command expects an environment file to work.  For this particular file you could use `conda create -n testingenv --file spec-file.txt`.
If you want to create an environemt file, I would suggest you to use the command `conda env export > myenv.yml` and then you can create an environment using `conda env create --name testingenv -f myenv.yml`

This avoids unncecesary mistakes in leaving genetic disorders or known CNV-syndromes etc out of a gene list if a single causative gene is not known.

2. As discussed elsewhere, lets start with a crude filter on frequency in current DBs plus a size cutoff. Minute variants will not visualise too well anyway, and can be triaged from within Scout.

We ended up annotating frequencies with both Genmod and VEP, but only the ones from Genmod end up in the database. The bugfix I submitted the other month just prevented a crash while loading VCFs with ExAC-annotations from VEP. Would be great if it could use the VEP freqs though, to avoid the double annotation.
A practical ex. take fam 16011. if you select clinical filter the variants on MTFMT disappear. there is not reason for that.

this is due to the call to subprocess.check_output(["make", "print-config"])
line 69 in setup.py
when python setup.py is called by make, output of make print-config is the following
```
make[1]: Entering directory `/tmp/pysam-0.9.1.4/htslib'
LDFLAGS =
LIBHTS_OBJS = kfunc.o knetfile.o kstring.o bgzf.o faidx.o hfile.o hfile_net.o hts.o md5.o regidx.o sam.o synced_bcf_reader.o vcf_sweep.o tbx.o vcf.o vcfutils.o cram/cram_codecs.o cram/cram_decode.o cram/cram_encode.o cram/cram_external.o cram/cram_index.o cram/cram_io.o cram/cram_samtools.o cram/cram_stats.o cram/files.o cram/mFILE.o cram/open_trace_file.o cram/pooled_alloc.o cram/rANS_static.o cram/sam_header.o cram/string_alloc.o cram/thread_pool.o cram/vlen.o cram/zfio.o hfile_libcurl.o
LIBS = -lcurl -lcrypto
PLATFORM = Linux
make[1]: Leaving directory `/tmp/pysam-0.9.1.4/htslib'
```

make add "depth" of calls and make[1] breaks the split 

easy fix: edit setup.py and change 
```
subprocess.check_output(["make", "print-config"])
```

to 

```
subprocess.check_output(["make", "-s", "print-config"])

```

make will be silenced and will not output make[1]..... stuff.

[This local validation code](https://github.com/Azure/azure-cli/blob/79ab16490b17f7ca8d58f3e05c81f47315c2d4df/src/command_modules/azure-cli-appservice/azure/cli/command_modules/appservice/_validators.py#L11) is blocking Azure Web Apps for Containers from being listed or shown:

```python
FUNCTION_APP_TYPES = ['functionapp', 'functionapp,linux']
WEB_APP_TYPES = ['app', 'app,linux']


def validate_existing_function_app(cmd, namespace):
    _validate_existing_app(cmd.cli_ctx, namespace, FUNCTION_APP_TYPES)


def validate_existing_web_app(cmd, namespace):
    _validate_existing_app(cmd.cli_ctx, namespace, WEB_APP_TYPES)


def _validate_existing_app(cli_ctx, namespace, app_type):
    client = web_client_factory(cli_ctx)
    instance = client.web_apps.get(namespace.resource_group_name, namespace.name)
    if instance.kind not in app_type:  # pylint: disable=no-member
        raise CLIError('Usage Error: {} is not a correct app type'.format(namespace.name))
    setattr(namespace, 'app_instance', instance)
```

The `kind` for Web Apps for Containers is `'app,linux,container'`.  Any new web app types, e.g., web apps for containers and Azure Functions Containers, will appear "broken" from a CLI end user perspective until this array is updated to include the correct `kind` values.  Users will receive the `'Usage Error: {} is not a correct app type'` message for (at least) `webapp list` and `webapp show` if their webapp is container based.  Is there a more dynamic way to perform this client side validation?  Is this client side validation necessary?

def _get_conn(self, timeout=None):
        """
        Get a connection. Will return a pooled connection if one is available.

        If no connections are available and :prop:`.block` is ``False``, then a
        fresh connection is returned.

        :param timeout:
            Seconds to wait before giving up and raising
            :class:`urllib3.exceptions.EmptyPoolError` if the pool is empty and
            :prop:`.block` is ``True``.
        """
        conn = None
        print('Using AKs new poolmanager')
        # If this is a persistent connection, check if it got disconnected
        if conn and is_connection_dropped(conn):
            log.info("Resetting dropped connection: %s" % self.host)
            conn.close()
            if getattr(conn, 'auto_open', 1) == 0:
                # This is a proxied connection that has been mutated by
                # httplib._tunnel() and cannot be reused (since it would
                # attempt to bypass the proxy)
                conn = None

        return conn or self._new_conn()

def _get_conn(self, timeout=None):
        """
        Get a connection. Will return a pooled connection if one is available.

        If no connections are available and :prop:`.block` is ``False``, then a
        fresh connection is returned.

        :param timeout:
            Seconds to wait before giving up and raising
            :class:`urllib3.exceptions.EmptyPoolError` if the pool is empty and
            :prop:`.block` is ``True``.
        """
        conn = None
        try:
            conn = self.pool.get(block=self.block, timeout=timeout)

        except AttributeError:  # self.pool is None
            raise ClosedPoolError(self, "Pool is closed.")

        except Empty:
            if self.block:
                raise EmptyPoolError(self,
                                     "Pool reached maximum size and no more "
                                     "connections are allowed.")
            pass  # Oh well, we'll create a new connection then

        # If this is a persistent connection, check if it got disconnected
        if conn and is_connection_dropped(conn):
            log.info("Resetting dropped connection: %s" % self.host)
            conn.close()
            if getattr(conn, 'auto_open', 1) == 0:
                # This is a proxied connection that has been mutated by
                # httplib._tunnel() and cannot be reused (since it would
                # attempt to bypass the proxy)
                conn = None

        return conn or self._new_conn()

We've considered a warning system in the past but it always had the
following problems:

## what if the warning is right?

It's too late, the push / commit has already happened.

Consider this hook:
- is using pre-push (for commit the problem is slightly less bad but still
requires local history rewriting)
- someone configures in ~/.boto instead
- accidentally commits creds
- warning fires in git push
- :(

## how does a hook communicate a warning

stdout / stderr don't really work since pre-commit combines them and treats
them as opaque bytes (this avoids the various encoding issues that the
hooks themselves may have)

return codes don't really work either since there's both no standard and
xargs gets involved

signals: not cross platform

## well it could be interactive

Currently pre-commit is entirely non interactive and I'd like to keep it
that way.  Reading input from the keyboard also poses issues for usage in
CI and personally I find prompts somewhat annoying. It'd also increase
warning fatigue especially if you have to answer a set of prompts every
time you commit.

## SKIP covered every use case for warnings we could think of

## opt-in vs opt-out

I've found that if you make a useful system that's opt in people are much
less likely to use it.  SKIP represents a (safer) opt out solution to the
problem

I recommend re-opening this issue and re-thinking the decision made. My reason for this is, that with Python 3.5, we got the: "async with" syntax for asynchronous context managers. Session lifetime is determined by the time span between calling the __aenter__ coroutine and calling the __aexit__ coroutine. On the other hand, session managers can be created by outside context synchronously.

See PEP 492 and the examples in there. Especially this one:
    async with lock:

"lock" is (synchronously) created by outside called, while "async with" (which then calls lock.__aenter__) is only callable from within the event loop. Actually, "async with lock" can be executed many times for the same lock.

The same should be possible for aiohttp. So aiohttp.ClientSession() should do nothing but create a ClientSessionManager. Then the __aenter__ method of the ClientSessionManager ought to do all the necessary resource acquisition (namely create a connector), and __aexit__ should tidy up again. It should also be possible to call __aenter__ and __aexit__ repeatedly, so __aenter__ should reopen a previously closed ClientSession, respectively create a new one. Only the object returned from __aenter__ should allow methods like request() or get().

Note, that you already follow the asynchronous session manager semantics for methods like request() or get(): Both request() and get() are synchronous methods, that do nothing but create a requestContextManager. In particular, they don't yield or await anything. They just store a reference to the coroutine, that will later - during the call to _aenter__ - asynchronously create the connection.

In my opinion, the same semantics, that get() and request() use, should also be used for aiohttp.ClientSession()

So, the following SHOULD work fine in my opinion:

class aiohttp_user:
    def __init__(self):
        self.clientsession = aiohttp.ClientSession()

    async def method(self, url):
        async with self.clientsession as client:
            async with cient.get(url) as request:
                ...

So, if some of the connections inside method() fail due to nasty server behaviour (timeouts, wrong answers etc.), the surrounding code can later retry calling method(), which will then re-start with a fresh connection pool, but otherwise with saved state (like cookies, partially retrieved data etc.).


In many cases, classes, that use aiohttp will be session managers themselves. Implementing that looks a bit ugly in Python:

class aiohttp_user:
    def __init__(self):
        self.clientsession = aiohttp.ClientSession()

    def __aenter__(self):
        try:
            self.client = await self.clientsession.__aenter__()
        catch:
            # tidy up, whatever needs to be tidied up (__aexit__ won't be called automtically!)
            throw

    def __aexit__(self, *args, **kwargs):
        flag = self.clientsession.__aexit__(*args, **kwargs)
        # Do further tidying up
        return flag

    async def method(self, url):
        async with cient.get(url) as request:
            ...

Currently, the above code doesn't work: It yields the nasty warning, that the whole discussion here is about, and it will even fail, if an object generated from aiohttp_user is used twice or more times inside "async with".

When changing the code, please keep in mind, that long-term ressources (like CookieJar etc.) should be bound to the persistent, non-async ClientSession (which is actually a ClientSessionManager), while volatile ressources (like the actual socket connection pool etc.) should be bound to the object, that is created upon call to ClientSession.__aenter__().

I don't understand the motovation for this PR. Master currently has a very clean set of abstractions that encode the idea of a cursor or active cell (selected) and a set of cells that are the target of an action (marked). These two abstractions are needed because of how selected is inextricably tied to the browsers notion of focus. The only issue we have right now in master is the awkward functioning of the shift-j/k keyboard shortcuts and the `extend_marked` method those use. Let's just fix that.

This PR introduces a third abstraction (soft selected) that conflates and confuses all of this in a really awkward way. And the changes are not just implementation details that fix the shift-j/k shortcuts. It mixes it with new concepts and interactions in the UI that are overly complex.

From the beginning of when @jdfreder and I started to talk about the marked cell concept, I was aware we would have to think carefully about how shift-j/k work. My design of the marked cells included background threads thinking about it.

I see two ways of fixing it:

* Remove the shift-j/k shortcuts and just support a Gmail style multiple style multiple selection. It is dead simple, conceptually clear and trivial to implement. It is also efficient if we pick a good shortcut for toggling marked.
* Keep shift-j/k but add a simple state machine to the `extend_marked` methods that gives the canonical behavior. I think we could get most of the way there by simply looking at the marked state of the current cell before extending and using that information. We may need a notion of an anchor that these methods manage, but I am not at all convinced of that. But even if we do that should be an internal detail of these methods that doesn't propagate to the rest of the API or the user.

According to the documentation in indy-node, the CLI implemented there has commands to perform the following actions, which are currently missing from the CLI in this repository:

- load a JSON file containing a connection request (`load <file>`)
- show a connection / connection request (`show connection <name>`)
- accept a connection request (`accept request from <name>`)
- check secure agent-to-agent communication channel (`ping <name>`)
- show a claim from the wallet or available from a remote agent (`show claim <name>`)
- obtain a claim from a remote agent (`request claim <name>`)
- show a proof request (`show proof request <name>`)
- send a proof to a remote agent (`send proof <name> to <name>`)

Most of these commands require the implementation of agent-to-agent communication, which is implemented in indy-node/indy_client (reusing some code from indy-plenum), but not in libindy.

As to your question, you need the cost function (and anything it calls that uses numpy code) to use autograd's wrapped numpy. The easiest fix would be to change the import statement in my_module.py.

As a hack, your code could munge the numpy module before importing `my_module`, as in

```python
import autograd.numpy as np
from autograd import grad

import numpy as unwrapped_numpy
unwrapped_numpy.log = np.log
from my_module import cost
```

The import of `my_module` has to happen after munging the loaded numpy module in case any numpy functions are bound directly to names in my_module.py, i.e. in case there are lines in my_module.py like `from numpy import log`. If the code in my_module.py only uses `import numpy as np` and references numpy functions through that module reference, then the import order doesn't matter.

This hack is not a good long-term recipe, and it might even depend on CPython module loading details. (I didn't test it, but something along these lines should work.)

I think this actually breaks more use cases than we realized.

```python
import autograd.numpy as np
from autograd.util import flatten

def f(x, y):
  xy = flatten([x, y])
  return np.sum(xy)

print grad(f)(1.0, 2.0)  # Exception: Don't know how to flatten type <type 'list'>
```

The issue is that there is no vspace for `[ArrayNode, ArrayNode]` for the usual reason.

Unless there's a simpler fix that I'm missing, I think the best solution might be to roll back this change and re-introduce the `flatten` with explicit recursion. (We could then also revert 8454fcf.)

Any thoughts @j-towns?

We don't currently have a good solution for this issue. We thought about it at one point, which led to the [`primitive_with_aux`](https://github.com/HIPS/autograd/blob/613176b3ca069e2ec6d3c78f2ad93698ce7cf84a/autograd/core.py#L150-L188) class, and I used that class for [exactly the kind of HMM caching that you're talking about](https://github.com/mattjj/svae/blob/eb922ea495ef46bd76c4648a43193fb729bd116f/svae/lds/cython_lds_inference.pyx). However, the `primitive_with_aux` strategy is incomplete because it breaks higher-order differentiation: the auxiliary values may themselves have to be boxed when we want to compute the grad of a grad.

(On a side note, if you want fast primitives for differentiating through HMM message passing routines, [my SVAE repo](https://github.com/mattjj/svae) has some of those, though not a complete set.)

We should leave this issue open until we get a better `primitive_with_aux` strategy. Maybe one exists in the new core branch.

Hi @marquesds, thank you for opening an issue!

It is correct - and intentional - the HTTP status code is suppressed: the clients/SDKs are meant to abstract away (transport) details like that. As described in the [documentation](https://developers.messagebird.com/docs/errors), everything you need to know can be derived from MessageBird's error codes. For example: if the resource is not found, we'll return HTTP 404, but the response will also have an error with code `20`.

@simonklb , @johnsca 

Sorry for not following this discussion more closely, I'm incredibly busy at the moment. I wanted to give further explanation as to why state machines are not the right approach for this.

**tl;dr: The "complete" state of an application in `charms.reactive` is a combination of different sub-states. Handlers do not react to the "complete" state of an application, but to a subset of the real state. This is something you can't model with state machines since state machines only model the "global/complete" state of an application, not what sub-states that global state is made of.**

A state machine makes sense if you can "model" changes to an application as a lifecycle. This makes some sense for a single monolythic applications. `installed`, `configured`, `started` are three states in the lifecycle of an application. You can only go to `started` if the application is `configured`.

However, you get issues when

 a) an application consists of multiple components
 b) applications have complex dependencies on each other 

The issues arise because a state machine machines can only have **one** state at a time. This means that an if an application consists of a webserver and an application server, the application can be in 4 states:

1. Nothing is installed
2. Only Webserver is installed
3. Only Application server is installed
4. Both are installed.

In fact, if you have an application that consists of `n` components that can be independently installed, you'll have 2^n possible states.

Juju is designed to solve `b)`, Juju is designed for "big software" that consists of lots and lots of services that depend on each other. This is why Juju is so great in the Openstack ecosystem. `charms.reactive` is (in part) designed to solve `a)`: many application share a common base, every charm author had to re-implement that base, there were no good ways to collaborate around that shared base.

So basically, using state machines is completely the opposite of what Juju is trying to do. Almost all conventional config management tools use lifecycles and state machines, and that is why they all fall apart on "big software". The industry is moving towards microservices, which means more of `b)`, which means conventional config mgmt tools will fall apart even more.

The fact that it can't be a state machine means that **we have to be really careful with thinking about this as a graph with transitions.** Because:

1) Multiple nodes in the graph will be active at the same time (multiple flags are active)
2) Transitioning from one node in the graph to another will not necessarily make the previous node inactive. (handlers don't remove the flags that are their preconditions.) [which is why this also isn't a petri-net]
3) We don't know what the complete graph looks like at design-time (each layer adds more stuff to the graph)

I hope this makes it clear that you cannot compare flags to states in a state machine (or in their respective graphs). Anyway, thanks a lot for this discussion and for trying stuff out. I hope I can find some time soon to go deeper into your proposals.

How about we split this PR up, one PR per common function? I want to see the common function and the call site changes in a single PR. That is very important to me because it's the only way we can be reasonably sure that the function signature works for all or at least a majority of the pipelines.

We could keep the build infrastructure changes (jenkins.sh) here but exclude the actual functions before merging. Or we could merge the infrastructure changes with the first per-function PR.

wrt the right way to write plugins - you declare the makers in the host project, and plugins import them from there

Are the names that you want the certs for publicly resolvable?

Will there be a server that responds to those names that can at least temporarily be contacted by the public during the issuance process?

If both of these things are true, it should be possible to use Let's Encrypt. If not, probably not; Let's Encrypt can only certify things that it can directly verify (by connecting to hosts referred to by the subject names in the certificate, or otherwise confirming public records related to these names).

Let's Encrypt will definitely not issue certs whose existence or contents needs to remain secret. All of the certs that the CA issues will be published.

As usual you've got everything right :)

> I assume that I can just leave these alone and the defaults will be reasonable for me. 

Typically yes.

> But I could not make sense of queued.*

Messages are pre-fetched from the broker and inserted into a local queue (fetchq) that the application serves by calling poll().
Since messages are fetched in batches/messagesets (which are defined by the broker) we might get anything from 1 to thousand of messages per Fetch request and they all end up in the local fetchq.
The queued.min.messages and queued.max.messages.kbytes controls whether the fetcher should request a given partition again, or wait until the fetchq falls below those thresholds to start fetching again.

@abalkin I'm trying to make an implementation here that has the post-495 rules, and I'm noticing that `fromutc` seems to get the wrong value if I have the DST value for "unspecified" default to DST instead of STD, and I'm fairly certain it's because `fromutc` is assuming that ambiguous dates return 0 for DST.

This is because in [CPython's `fromutc`](https://github.com/python/cpython/blob/master/Lib/datetime.py#L976), it basically creates a datetime that is offset by 1 DST offset on the DST side if the correct output is on the DST side, and a datetime that is the correct value but _ambiguous_ if the correct output is on the STD side. If my code interprets unspecified ambiguous dates as being on the DST side, `fromutc()` will then be off by 1 hour:

(using my current working directory, with ambiguous dates defaulting to the DST side)
```python
>>> from datetime import datetime, timedelta
>>> from dateutil.tz import tzlocal, tzutc
>>> EST = tzlocal()
>>> UTC = tzutc()
>>> dt_edt_utc = datetime(2016, 11, 6, 5, 30, tzinfo=UTC)   # Unambiguously EDT
>>> dt_edt = dt_edt_utc.astimezone(EST)
>>> dt_edt, dt_edt.tzname()
(datetime.datetime(2016, 11, 6, 1, 30, tzinfo=tzlocal()), 'EDT')
>>> dt_est = (dt_edt_utc + timedelta(hours=1)).astimezone(EST)   # Should be 1:30 EST
>>> dt_est
datetime.datetime(2016, 11, 6, 2, 30, tzinfo=tzlocal())
```

Since it seems you've tackled this before, do you have any recommendation here?

We could add caching to slightly decrease load on the database and we could change the design of getting upload urls so that a pool is used for sync and then tokens are put back in the database once, after the uploading is finished.
That becomes complicated though and it doesn't really solve the problem reliably, doesn't maximize the performance.

Some time ago we have considered and dropped a design, where a daemon is spawned on the client machine. This daemon process then accepts connections on localhost tcp port and actually performs operations like uploading, downloading, encrypting etc. Such design resolves the problem of sharing state between multiple processes, that we now support - a client process needs to access the database only once, to determine if the daemon is currently running and on which port. The rest of the operations would be handled by the daemon, which could keep almost everything in memory and only flush things to down to the database when it is shutting down.
The daemon design didn't make much sense when we discussed it before, but now that we are looking into encryption and possibly compression, I thought about a few more issue that the daemon could resolve:
* (performance of database access, as mentioned above)
* depending on compressability and the source device speed, we can hit the CPU bottleneck with hashing, compression and encryption. Once the daemon is up, moving file uploading into a separate *process* would be relatively easy and it would allow usage of more than one CPU on the client machine
* if the user has considerable amount of data to upload and download, flow control can significantly reduce the total time required for the entire job to finish. For example, if we have 100 files to upload and 100 to download, with 2*N threads, then we want N threads to saturate the upload link and the other N threads to saturate the download link
* if we have centralized daemon configuration, we can limit the throughput of upload, download and local io
* if the user has a HDD device, we could trade a bit of memory to optimize for speed. HDDs are quite fast in sequential access and quite slow in random access. By reading/writing from a buffer, we can (maybe) turn the multi-process race to the hard drive into an orderly queue, decreasing utilization of the device and increasing performance (if the device was a bottleneck). (this is not the first thing I would implement)

I don't get why people are so concerned about naming and claim that it is used a lot. It should not. Most common - "password" is load_only, "id" - dump_only. If you have use cases that require you defining complex dependencies and load/dump schemas, you should really consider splitting them into a separate schemas (maybe with reusing common parts through base schema). [@validates](https://github.com/zzzeek/sqlalchemy/blob/f1920d2713fc43cfa7089e7bd5592908e90fd69b/lib/sqlalchemy/orm/mapper.py#L2877) methods eventually get wrapped in the [utils._validator_events()](https://github.com/zzzeek/sqlalchemy/blob/fdb6ab6a1d5d55d900c388e039835c6433032977/lib/sqlalchemy/orm/util.py#L81) event handler.

Deferring attribute resolution to the validator seems to implicitly allow overriding inherited attributes without overriding their validators, which I'm not sure is desirable.

I have a suspicion that this API is an artifact of it being easier to pass the model instance and field name to the validator method than trying to resolve a model class's column to a model instance's column (which marshmallow already does).

For historical context, https://github.com/zzzeek/sqlalchemy/commit/3829b89 contains the original implementation of the `@validates` decorator. As far as I can tell, passing the attribute name was the way an example suggested doing it, then it was copied to a core feature.

True, in that context it would make sense to exclude `dump_only` fields from unmarshalling result with `INCLUDE`, but it would make even more sense to raise as @deckar01 suggests.

So, should we consider `dump_only` fields as _known_ and *disallowed*? That would mean that `unknown` option would be irrelevant (be it `INCLUDE`/`RAISE`/`EXCLUDE`) because we are not handling an unknown field, but rather known with contract (being read-only). So with any `unknown` value, marshmallow should raise error (_"Field is read-only."_)?

Or did @deckar01 mean that `EXCLUDE` would _not_ raise error if `dump_only` field is given? I think it would be better and more consistent to raise also then (never allow assinging value to a read-only field)?

> Being able to ignore dump_only fields

If `dump_only` is intended for _read-only_ fields, you should never want to ignore them? Just fail fast - if client provides those fields, give error (or client will _think_ that the field it sent actually had impact).

You raise fair points @davidism.

The rationale for the current API is as follows:

- The Schema decorators--`preprocessor` and `data_handler`--allow multiple Schemas to use the same pre- and post-processing routines without having to inherit from base classes or mixins. They also hide the fact that multiple functions can be stored within a list.
- `make_object` is a method because is is meant to be specific to a Schema. Whereas pre- and post-processors are written as generic functions, `make_object` provides a 1-to-1 mapping between a schema and an "app-level" object.
- There is no preprocessing hook for dumping/serialization because marshmallow is not meant to make updates to app-level objects. All processing happens in your application's business logic *before* serialization.

Here is the intended flow:

```
[client input] -> preprocess/validate -> make object -> [app-level object] -> business logic* -> [app-level-object] -> serialize -> modify serialized data
```

That said, I am not entirely opposed to changing the API if it is unclear. Perhaps you could describe your use case in more detail and we can discuss possible solutions.

Something like this maybe:

```
+ alfred *{weight=100}
- <@>

+ * alfred{weight=100}
- <@>
```

The high weight value should ensure the trigger gets tested _first_ (unless you have other triggers with weight >100). If it begins or ends with the word "alfred" it just redirects to a trigger without that word.

It may be possible to combine them into one trigger, but using optionals on either end like your original example might backfire: the first iteration you'd match the `[alfred] * [alfred]` trigger and it would match _whether or not_ you said the word alfred (because it's optional, you see), and it would redirect to `<star>` and it would test `[alfred] * [alfred]` first again, and repeat forever.

```python
@classmethod
    def build_json_resource_obj(cls, fields, resource, resource_instance, resource_name,
                                force_type_resolution=False):
        # Determine type from the instance if the underlying model is polymorphic
        if force_type_resolution:
            resource_name = utils.get_resource_type_from_instance(resource_instance)
        resource_data = [
            ('type', resource_name),
            ('id', encoding.force_text(resource_instance.pk) if resource_instance else resource.get('id')),
            ('attributes', cls.extract_attributes(fields, resource)),
        ]
        relationships = cls.extract_relationships(fields, resource, resource_instance)
        if relationships:
            resource_data.append(('relationships', relationships))
        # Add 'self' link if field is present and valid
        if api_settings.URL_FIELD_NAME in resource and \
                isinstance(fields[api_settings.URL_FIELD_NAME], relations.RelatedField):
            resource_data.append(('links', {'self': resource[api_settings.URL_FIELD_NAME]}))
        return OrderedDict(resource_data)
```

> I think "the number of axes not to be flattened" is the wrong way to think about it, because everything gets flattened into a matrix

No, I'm actually suggesting to *not* flatten it into a matrix. With `keep_axes=2`, it would flatten a tensor of `(2,3,4,5)` into a tensor of `(2,3,20)`, and then `T.dot()` against a weight matrix of `(20,30)` would result in a `(2,3,30)` output. There's no need to turn it into a `(6,20)` matrix for that:
```
>>> import numpy as np
>>> import theano
>>> import theano.tensor as T
>>> a = T.dtensor3()
>>> b = T.dmatrix()
>>> T.dot(a, b).eval({a: np.random.randn(2,3,20), b: np.random.randn(20,30)}).shape
(2, 3, 30)
```
This would allow to stack a `DenseLayer(..., num_units=..., keep_axes=-1)` directly on top of a recurrent layer and get results in a `(batchsize, seq_len, num_units)` tensor. Incidentally, Keras has a separate layer class for this use case (the `TimeDistributedDense` class).

> This is very different from e.g. FlattenLayer's `outdim` argument, which directly controls how many trailing axes get flattened

I'd rather say the `outdim` argument does this *indirectly*. It *directly* gives the number of output dimensions. Anyway, thinking along this line, we could also give the `DenseLayer` an argument telling how many trailing axes to involve in the dot product. Then, semantics would be as follows:
* Positive integer: Give the number of trailing axes to collapse.
* Negative integer: Give the (negated) number of leading axes to keep.
* Zero: Either error out or collapse input to a vector.

The default would be `-1`. Semantics would be exactly inverse to what I proposed before, but maybe this would make naming easier?

> As for the name, I think I prefer something with `distribute`, or maybe something else altogether, but `keep_axes` is not clear enough.

Hooray, a name discussion! :)

Thanks for your suggestion! Unfortunately, it has a small problem: The learning rate is usually adapted after a fixed number of updates (e.g., an epoch), not after every single update, so this has to be maintained in the training loop and not in the update dictionary.

If you really want to decay the learning rate after each update, it's as simple as:
```python
eta = theano.shared(lasagne.utils.floatX(initial_eta))
updates = lasagne.updates.sgd(loss, params, learning_rate=eta)
updates[eta] = eta * 0.999
```

Or:
```python
eta = theano.shared(lasagne.utils.floatX(initial_eta))
eta.default_update = eta * 0.999
updates = lasagne.updates.sgd(loss, params, learning_rate=eta)
```

This would not really get any simpler by providing a wrapper (and as I said, the use would be limited, since you'd typically not change the rate after every update step). Automating this only makes sense once (and if) we add support for a training loop. Until then, you should just manually decay the learning rate in your training loop:
```python
eta = theano.shared(lasagne.utils.floatX(initial_eta))
updates = lasagne.updates.sgd(loss, params, learning_rate=eta)
...
for ... in ...:
    for ... in ...:
        ... = train_fn(...)
    eta.set_value(eta.get_value() * lasagne.utils.floatX(0.98))
```
Hope this helps!

PS: You should go through the [Theano tutorial](http://deeplearning.net/software/theano/tutorial/), or the [extended Lasagne tutorial](http://f0k.de/lasagne-embl/) -- you fell for a common misunderstanding about what is executed when in symbolic programming. In particular, your `self.learning_rate = some change to the rate` would only be executed once, not after every update.

> the path stepping is wrong

This is expected, since there is no way for a path to know whether a call is a tail call or not. As @rhelmot said, If you specify `CALLLESS`, it will jump all `call` instructions, regardless of the real intent of that call instruction. CFGAccurate knows it slightly better because it has global information through CFG recovery and some graph analysis.

A workaround is to detect such cases and disable `CALLLESS` option before reaching the basic block where that instruction 0x4016fa belongs.

Alternatively, for already widely-used packages we could include the citation information directly with due_credit, so that something like

duecredit_cite_('GNU Octave')

would automatically use the correct information that is part of duecredit.

Then, when run from Matlab / Octave, if the users calls 

```
duecredit_cite()

Best place for this sort of question is possibly the new discuss forums: https://discuss.dronekit.io/

> Can I switch to loiter mode, perform what I want on the RP and then switch it back to auto, so it continues with a mission? I read the pixhawk will then restart the mission.

Yes. You should perform the RP code in GUIDED mode (not Loiter). The pixhawk will either resume or restart the mission when you switch back to AUTO depending on the vehicle parameters (can't remember which one - look it up). Whether you restart, resume, or load a completely different mission is up to you.

In addition to sending commands in guided mode, you can in theory also dynamically create a mission in AUTO mode and upload the modified mission when needed. 

> Is there a way of doing it without running the groundstation from the raspberry pi ?

Probably not. You can't run your control script on the pixhawk itself so the RaPi is the only computing platform you have on board. While you can run the script from a PC say and communicate with the Pixhawk over a telemetry link, that is usually too high-latency (slow) for image processing work.

What are you envisaging?

I think, if python 2.6 is the default system version then I can expect that it works, including a decent set of modules which I expect to be installed (although no "pip", of course).
That's a different story for any additional / alternative python2.7 which may come from an "unknown source" (e.g. compiled by somebody without doing any comprehensive tests, be this way or another broken, missing modules, ...).

> I've always been a bit fuzzy on licensing issues, but as far as I know people can re-use your code if it's GPL, they just can't turn around and charge for it (and they have to give credit where credit is due). I have no plans to ever switch cpptraj's license from GPL.

Actually, I can take cpptraj and turn around and sell it.  I can make small changes and sell it as a "new product" (albeit, derived from cpptraj with you retaining the copyright for the parts you wrote and me for my modifications).  However, what the GPL *doesn't* let me do is relicense it using another, more restrictive license.  Which means that if someone wants to buy it from me and I sell it to them, I *must* provide the source code.  And they now have the right to do with it everything that I had the right to do with it.  So while I can sell it, they can give it away for free, and I can't stop them.

BSD and MIT, as I understand it, gives you the right to relicense it as you want (but obviously the original licenses still exist, so if you take BSD or MIT and license it under a closed license, the open licenses still exist).  And if you link to a piece of GPL code, your whole project becomes derived and, therefore, GPL itself.  LGPL lets you keep just the library under an open license and keep the rest closed.

The reason people like Linus Torvalds likes the GPL (v2 -- he dislikes v3 for Linux) is because it basically requires that you make any modifications available upstream (unless you don't release it to *anybody*).  It seems fair that if I release something for free, and person A makes an improvement, I shouldn't be locked out of obtaining that improvement and effectively being forced out of my own project.

> And then I found the region of this match is 'EUW', which means the region setting is useless?

It sounds like this is a current limitation of Cass. When a summoner changes regions, their match history stays with them (e.g. I move my NA account to EUW, then my NA matches will still remain in my match history from the API). Since Cass is relying on a region parameter set by the user, then it isn't taking this into account.

You could /probably/ get around this by temporarily changing the region to the match's region, then changing it back (depends on how your processes are setup). 

I believe support for this is being planned for the next version, but I'm not certain of an ETA or anything on that.

Hey Markus,

Thanks for opening the PR and this issue and sorry for replying so late.
I agree that the way connections are done now have a lot of things to improve, and actually I like most of the ideas that you have in the PR.

However before moving forward with it I think is worth to analyze how connections should work, trying to solve questions like:
* Where the connection resolution should live? Should we start transferring some resolver logic to the Connection type?
* How we should handle the "automatic" connection types creation? We should handle it at all? (as is right now)
* How we can handle different connections for same types? And then, how we handle name clashing?

Some of this questions are easy to solve if we are more explicit (as your PR points), but before choosing a definitive approach would be good to explore if there is any way that we could achieve simplicity and extensibility at the same time (as we did for `Node`s).

In general I think is worth to spend some time analyzing, discussing and getting a stable API for connections than releasing something that we might have to change after.

Please raise any ideas you have! (and thanks for your great work too!)

PS: Meanwhile I'm going to add support to the test you pointed to unblock your transition to `1.0` :)

Hey,

Chipping in with how I've implemented code page handling in this project's PHP sibling.

I've got these languages working: Danish, German, Greek, English, Spanish, French, Irish Gaelic, Hungarian, Icelandic, Japanese (Katakana only at this stage), Latvian, Polish, Russian, Turkish and Vietnamese. Feel free to adapt my unit tests if this approach sounds useful to you.

This approach is not sufficient for scripts that don't fit in half a code page. Users have had mixed results with Thai, and Chinese support is via a printer-specific hack, and I was unable to get the contextual forms of Arabic letters to display. In addition, Hebrew and Arabic also print backwards, Japanese support is incomplete (printer issues), and Indian scripts have not been attempted at all.

### Data representation

When printing, the driver needs to know:

- Which code pages I can find characters in. This is stored as a map of UTF-8 characters to lists of code page numbers (called `$available`)
- How to convert the characters to the target code page. For each code page, this is stored as a map of UTF-8 characters to the characters in the target code page (called `$encode`)

The data sources here are:

- `iconv` output, allowing checks for what's in each code page
- The printer model properties, containing lists of code pages ([example](https://github.com/mike42/escpos-php/blob/f6bb510e6f3668ae278d7aec3afb029104e1459f/src/Mike42/Escpos/CapabilityProfiles/DefaultCapabilityProfile.php), [other example](https://github.com/mike42/escpos-php/blob/cfea4c4fc95516ac953e1e5b623f854632afa2ee/src/Mike42/Escpos/CapabilityProfiles/StarCapabilityProfile.php))

### Algorithm

- The driver reads as long as it can find that the characters exist in the current code page (checking that the current code page is on the `$available` list for the character).
- When it finds something it can't encode, it adds a "?".
- It puts the text through a conversion from UTF-8 to the current code page (using the `$encode` table for the code page), and sends it off to the printer.
- When it finds something in another code page, it changes code page and repeats.

### Implementation

The implementation of building this data structure and working with it is:

- [EscposPrintBuffer.php](https://github.com/mike42/escpos-php/blob/cfea4c4fc95516ac953e1e5b623f854632afa2ee/src/Mike42/Escpos/PrintBuffers/EscposPrintBuffer.php)

The unit tests are more interesting though:

- [EscposPrintBufferTest.php](https://github.com/mike42/escpos-php/blob/f6bb510e6f3668ae278d7aec3afb029104e1459f/test/unit/EscposPrintBufferTest.php)

Hi @nlothian,

Thank you for reporting this.

This is likely to be a tokenization error, because fastText does not recognize the space character as such. For example, in your second case, the space is actually a "thin space" (UTF decimal code point 8201) which is not recognized by fastText as a space. One way to solve the issue is to preprocess your data using `sed`:
```
> sed 's/[[:space:]]/ /g' < data.txt > preprocessed.data.txt
```

We will work on a fix for this.

Best,
Edouard.

I think this is a critical issue. Earlier this year, I spent many hours tuning hyperparams, and didn't have the compute resources available to waste time on poor performing models. Eventually, I had to reduce my parameter space to a very simple space (which cost me at least two months of research effort). > to evaluate stopping criteria and compute optimial next guesses. [Hyperband] treats hyperparam optimization as a resource allocation problem, not one of fitting + optimizing some function. They try to speed up random evaluation of hyperparams and do not try to perform any sort of optimization. They do this by devoting compute time to well-performing models. This is possible by taking advantage of the iterative training process for ML models. At first, Hyperband trains many models for a few iterations. It then stops or "kills" some fraction of the worst performing and repeats. i.e., part of Hyperband trains 100 models for 1 iteration, then the best 50 models for 2 iterations, then the best 25 models for 4 iterations and so on. The theory backing this reformulation (the [multi-armed bandit problem][2]) is natural and well-studied (but takes effort to apply it here). They show it's competitive with other Bayesian methods (spearmint, TPE, SMAC) as well as random search, and also random search with twice the computational budget in figures 8 (which I think is their most realistic example): I think the input of computational budget not configs to evaluate makes it even more attractive. I'm planning on working to verify any gains, then implementing this algorithm in Dask. I'll be on this soon: I'll be more free after May 4th, and certainly after the semester ends. [Hyperband]:https://arxiv.org/abs/1603.06560 [2]:https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Multi-armed_bandit

C++ default arguments are really syntactic sugar: that code only defines one constructor with 4 arguments; when you leave off the last one or two in a constructor, the compiler generates a construction call with the defaults added in.

So with that in mind, pybind can't know that there are default arguments there, it has to be told, which is what `py::arg` (or the `_a` annotation) are for:

http://pybind11.readthedocs.io/en/stable/basics.html#default-args

(Also note that defaults specified this way don't have to agree with the defaults specified in C++: that is, your python interface could have default values for mandatory C++ arguments or vice versa; the defaults given by `py::arg` can also be different than those specified by C++).

> --expand has to at least take the arguments to the -d flag, right? And then it looks for a matching cert?

Sorry I should have worded that better. You're correct that you have to provide domains when using `--expand`. Certbot just errored when running the command `certbot --expand my-cert -d d.com,e.com` written above because I think you forgot `--cert-name` before my-cert.

> certbot --cert-name my-cert.org -d b,org,c.net,d.com, e.com will have the effect of deleting a.com, preserving b.org and c.net, and adding d.com and e.com to the cert "my-cert.org". It gives exact control over the domains handled by a cert whose name you know.

Correct!

> certbot --expand -d b,org,c.net,d.com, e.com will look for a cert that has the most domains in common with the list in -d (in this case hopefully it finds my-cert.org) and then makes the same changes (in this example) as the previous --cert-name example.

So the way the cert is searched for with `--expand`, it only considers certs where all domains in the cert are also included on the command line. In this example, a.com isn't on the command line, but is in the cert so my-cert is ignored because the domains you specified aren't a strict "expansion" of the existing cert. Unless you have another certificate that only contains domains included on the command line, Certbot just creates a new lineage!

> That is, the difference between --expand and --cert-name is mainly how it finds the cert, and then the domains specified in -d become the new set of domains for the next version of the cert in the lineage.

You are correct that `--expand` and `--cert-name` find the cert differently and after running the command, the domains specified become the names in the certificate obtained. The problem identified with `--expand` as seen in your example is although the cert you obtained had the domains specified, you didn't expand anything and Certbot just made you a new lineage.

Hopefully that makes sense! I'm happy to answer more questions if you have them.

All the requested changes have been made, and the problem with Fedora 23 was happening because update_includes() didn't prepopulate the includes list with the `Include` and `IncludeOptional` statements found from the actual configuration prior to doing the `apachectl -t -D DUMP_INCLUDES`.

The binary configuration dump would then add specific files found to the Augeas tree (eg: `/path/to/file1.conf` and `/path/to/file2.conf`) and the actual iteration later would then add `/path/to/*.conf`. Some versions of Augeas, including the one shipped with Fedora 23 don't play well when loading a single file via multiple statements (*.conf AND file1.conf for example).

This was simply fixed by populating the parser paths first from the actual configuration file, which is the source of wildcard includes, while binary configuration dump gives fully resolved paths. The later additions check if path is already parsed, and doesn't add new ones to the list if so.

Take a look at how simple_salesforce logs in. It builds XML representing the body of the SOAP request to Salesforce's [login endpoint.](https://developer.salesforce.com/docs/atlas.en-us.api_asynch.meta/api_asynch/asynch_api_quickstart_login.htm) One of the parameters in this XML, required by Salesforce, is the password in plain text.

https://github.com/simple-salesforce/simple-salesforce/blob/364c889abe9e22b7145524f2dc422e41c5f0639b/simple_salesforce/login.py#L67

If you really wanted your password to be hashed in your code you can modify the source code or create a pull request for login.py to accept hashing by allowing the option to decrypt the hash with a given algorithm before it logs in. But depending on how you have your hash stored, that probably won't help too much since your hash will be available in plain text (or available somehow to your program) and so will the decryption algorithm you've chosen to use. So whoever is looking at your code would be able to decrypt it :P.

Assuming there is an attack vector in raiden's protocol for an amplification attack, how does a global throtle strategy for sent packets help?

So, the attacker sends `n` offeding messages to a number of nodes, N0 ... Nn, each of these nodes will send `a` new messages resulting in `a*n`, after each node `N` has sent 10 message they will hang. The throttling strategy doesn't prevent A from sending the initial message and for the nodes being attacked it will add a delay for sending *all* messages, in my opnion this makes the attack worse.

I suggest a per-address throttling at the *receiving* end instead of a global bucket for *sending*, but I don't think that throttling alone is sufficient to prevent this sort of attack.

Based on @CyrusNajmabadi's in-person and online feedback I've reworked the programming idioms here a bit.

`controlFlow` now is an enum with three variants:
* `Bail` - Indicates to the caller that the callee issued a diagnostic and does not think that the plan should continue.
* `Continue` - Indicates to the caller that the callee thinks the plan should continue. The callee *may still have issued diagnostics*, but the plan can still proceed despite it.
* `Undefined` - Indicates to the caller that the callee doesn't know what to do because it or something it calls transitively has hit an internal-to-Pulumi error (i.e. a bug). It's up to the caller to decide what to do.

A function returning a `controlFlow` returns two or three things:

1. First, if the function returns data (i.e. `[]Step`, as is the case with this PR), the first return value is the data.
2. The recommended control flow, one of `Bail`, `Continue`, or `Undefined`.
3. An `error`.

The returned data is not the default value, `controlFlow` must be `Continue` and `error` must be nil. Callers interpret this as "please continue, this operation was successful."

If `controlFlow` is `Bail`, the data must be the default value and `error` must be nil. Callers interpret this as "please do not continue, this operation was not successful but we have gracefully issued appropriate diagnostics."

If `controlFlow` is `Undefined`, the data must be the default value and `error` must not be nil. Callers interpret this as "figure out what to do next, this operation failed because of an internal error."

metric, so `neg_mean_loss` is still needed. Hope that clarifies things.

changing this - perhaps something like HyperOptAlg(metric="mean_loss",
direction=minimize") would be more intuitive?

I think we should stick to the current versioning schema, perhaps we should be more strict - but most releases add plugins which should increase the minor version, we hardly do any bug fix only releases. 

As for the specific issues, I have made some notes. 

[#8 revamp cli params / cli](https://github.com/streamlink/streamlink/issues/8), I think this is way too big to work on right now :)

[#134 Support VOD seeking](https://github.com/streamlink/streamlink/issues/134) is big, realistically I don't see that making it in for a 1.0.0 release :)

[#180 --player-external-http only supports one connection](https://github.com/streamlink/streamlink/issues/180), there hasn't been anything decided about _how_ this should work or what the user's expectation is. It might be assumed that multiple connections are simple as you just write the stream to multiple clients, and anyone joining the stream would be getting the same data as other clients. This will not work for most stream types and would need to be customised to support each stream type. Even HLS (MPEG-TS) would be tricky as you'd have to start the stream from the start of a segment. The only way I can see to implement this is to open a new stream for each client, so they wouldn't be synchronised and you'd use X times the number of bandwidth. 

[#573 cli: please remove deprecated warning messages](https://github.com/streamlink/streamlink/issues/573), I think we should just remove the deprecated messages and leave it as it is. The options are not included in the help or the documentation, the `--http-header` etc. options should be used instead but backwards compatibility is maintained. 

[#680 installer: build a Windows XP compatible installer](https://github.com/streamlink/streamlink/issues/680), I agree with @gravyboat that we should not have to support XP, it's a choice between supporting more users and not supporting (philosophically, not technically) Windows XP.

[#916 Streamlink 0.6.0 fails to play streams on PotPlayer](https://github.com/streamlink/streamlink/issues/916) is important I think, it points to an underlying issue with the retries rather than an incompatibility with PotPlayer.

[#920 Add a Record option](https://github.com/streamlink/streamlink/issues/920), just needs some final review :)

[#966 Enable certificate verification for Twitch](https://github.com/streamlink/streamlink/issues/966), lets just turn it back on! 

[#1055 Fallback selection for --stream-sorting-excludes](https://github.com/streamlink/streamlink/issues/1055), I can see why @bastimeyer would be pushing for this one ;) It's a nice to have feature.  

I have created a [Project for 1.0.0](https://github.com/streamlink/streamlink/projects/4), we should add the issues we want resolved (either implementing them, or closing as `wont-fix`) for version 1.0.0. I have created 4 columns, `Screening`, `Todo`, `In Progress`, and `Done`. `Screening` should contain issues that we want to discuss and decide if they should go in to or be resolved before 1.0.0. `Todo` is issues that we have decided should be resolved for 1.0.0. `In Progress` and `Done` are pretty self-explanatory :) What are you guys' thoughts on this approach?

So the result of `execute()` changes according to the `process=True` arg ? I'm `-1` on that solution.
One time it's a bloking method, the other time, it's not. I think that's not a good design.
I think `.execute()` should never block, it should just send the query to the server, then a `fetchsome()` may receive the data with progress info. If the user don't care about progress, he will use `fetchall()`.
Lots of database API uses this kind of API.

- Here the DB API compliance (for inspiration): https://www.python.org/dev/peps/pep-0249/
- Here the fetch method of python sqlite: https://docs.python.org/3/library/sqlite3.html#sqlite3.Cursor.fetchone
- Here the fetch method of python postgresql: http://initd.org/psycopg/docs/cursor.html#cursor.fetchone

I know this is lots of modifications since `clickhouse-driver` was not designed like that, but I think it's a more common (and better) design.

@zsdonghao The new API is still capable of building such network, you just need to use it in the following way:

```python
n1 = Layer1()(x)
n2 = Layer2()(n1)
n3 = Layer3(n1.outputs.shape())(n2)
n4 = Layer4()(n3)
```

Basically, the new API isn't less expressive than the old one.
Every expression in the old API can be converted into an equivalent one in the new API, by just moving the first argument `net` to the end: `ALayer(net, ...)` -> `ALayer(...)(net)`.
So we won't drop anything when upgrading to the new API.

This needs clarification. The only standard library modules that should be in REQUIRED_MODULES and actually symlinked are those needed to bootstrap virtualenv's site.py. In other words, the failure mode for a missing module in REQUIRED_MODULES is the virtualenv's python failing to start because of a failed import in site.py. Once site.py runs and virtualenv starts up, the "global" stdlib directory should be on sys.path and it shouldn't matter what stdlib modules are symlinked into the virtualenv's lib directory.

Based on the symptom you describe, it seems like perhaps Django (or Django's test-suite) are relying on the specific location of those modules in a way that they shouldn't, or that it least is not virtualenv compatible (which is odd, since it clearly passes fine on python 2)? More detail on the failures needed.

> The API for Python 2 and 3 must be as close as possible, this will smooth the transition for developers moving from 2 to 3. And will keep the code simple, from the C API point of view PyUnicode is still PyUnicode, on Python 2 and 3. This invalidates the third approach.

It's going to be awkward at the C level no matter what you do. You're either going to end up having a bunch of stub methods that use the raw APIs to provide Unicode versions (increasing the amount of stuff you have to test), or you're going to have `#ifdef`s in the code (and potentially the tests).

I still think going for the native, encoding-agnostic approach on Python 2 makes the most sense. It'll be easier to interoperate with other Python 2 libraries (no worrying about `unicode` objects infecting `str` objects and doing implicit decoding). If you need a Unicode representation of the data in the repo, you're going to need to decide exactly how you want to do that anyway (because, again, there's no single, correct way to do it).

> Then, what happens if two developers with different locales work on the same project? It must work. So things like names in tree entries should be in UTF-8.

Let me just say up front: this is a general issue with Git (or even Unix). It's not a pygit2 issue.

But let's indulge this idea that it "must work." What does "working" mean? Let's define a few scenarios:

Scenario 1: You assume everything is UTF-8.

- What if there are non-UTF-8 filenames in the repo? Do they come out as garbage now?
- What happens if the current system's locale isn't UTF-8? Do I write out filenames that will appear to be garbage to every other program on the system?

Scenario 2: You assume everything is in the user's locale.

- What if there are filenames that don't match the locale's encoding? Do they come out as garbage too?
- Now I'm at least writing out data that can interoperate with the rest of the system. That might not interoperate with other systems, but the world hasn't agreed on and completely switched to one encoding, so that's a problem no matter what.

Scenario 3: You try to detect the encoding of everything.

- What if your detection is slightly wrong? What if it's totally wrong? What if it can't even figure out how to decode the filename? Do you do a lossy conversion?
- Do you do your encoding detection based on everything in the repository, or on things individually?
- How do you interoperate with any other program on the system? You may or may not have gotten things into a state where they look right. You may or may not have changed the strings in a way that prevents you from using them in other applications. You don't really know anything for certain unless you stick to the raw APIs.

Scenario 4: You devise some kind of system that always figures out the right thing to do.

Awesome. Except no other program is in on your scheme, so you're screwed anyway!

With mixed locale encodings, users will have problems across *every program they use anyway*. They're already screwed. The solution is to stop mixing encodings.

At the end of the day, sure, these are real problems. But they're problems for user-facing programs to solve, not problems for a low level library. The best thing you can do is try to do the same thing Python itself would do. In this case that involves doing everything based on the user's locale.
